The error you pasted is not saying the file does not exist; it is saying it can't decode a JSON object from the "highlight" parameter that should have been sent by the client.

Answers to these questions will help me to debug the issue:

1. Have you upgraded gdbgui recently, and did this start occurring after the upgrade? The backend is expecting the `highlight` parameter, which only newer client code sends (these two should always be in sync, I'm not sure how they'd get out of sync).  
1. When did it start happening? Does this always occur everytime gdbgui tries to open a file? 
1. If not, are you doing anything differently when the error occurs?
Another question: Are you using any character encoding overrides? Something other than utf-8?

Hey @johnarnold 

Agreed -- we have seen similar response/performance degradation on our system.

I was hoping to be able to switch the backend search implementation on a per-request basis. But if that's too challenging to accomplish in the current implementation, that's fine. I wanted it so I could compare time/performance benchmarks, without having to restart flower. My company has tried to use the persistence mode but we've had bad experience with it -- flower would fail to start because of reading the shelve.

I will open a PR to get some draft feedback. The sending to elasticsearch part will need to be refined for flower. Originally I wasn't sure if this would fit as a piece in flower proper, a flower subcommand, or some external script, but if flower is going to be using the index (configurable of course), then it I think it will be required to be part of flower in some scope. Given that, I've adjusted my initial implementation to call into the sender on event processing -- I'm sure this will be changed, but I wanted to show the general idea.

The schema mapping also will have to be put somewhere else. This really is a WIP.

Additionally, I'm not sure if it makes sense, but I could add in support for the dashboard page to pull from elasticsearch, too. Just not sure if that's the most wise thing for the project. History is one thing, but realtime dashboard -- let me know your thoughts.

Hi Janusz,

> I've commited greenlet support: https://github.com/LEW21/pydbus/commit/9d8d8c1d237cee70614e1df9f62e74ad7308f2fe
>
> As it turns out, it's really self-contained, and works quite well.

If greenlets are an option. For instance greenlets are incompatible with 
Stackless Python. And greenlets still have issues with GC and threads. 
But granted, for most cases greenlets work well.

> It can also be easily extended to support other backends, if there is a need for that.  Therefore, I don't really see a good use case for providing an async API.

Well, if you provide a middle-ware library like pydbus is, you can't 
expect to foresee all applications. For instance I'm in charge for a 
large distributed workflow system, that requires Stackless Python, 
because it serializes tasklets and moves them around between different 
computers. The application is compatible with GLib/Gio, but can't use 
greenlets. Fortunately it doesn't need DBus access. :-)

 > Python is a high-level language, and people should code like they're 
using a high-level language, not like they're writing C or JavaScript.

I don't think, it is a good idea to enforce a particular overall design 
for GLib based applications, just because they need to publish methods 
on DBus. Why to reduce the applicability of pydbus for no good technical 
reason?

> Also, maintaining an async API is not something I'm looking forward to.

That's a perfectly valid reason. Of course it is your time and it is 
really important, that you like your project. Otherwise it will end like 
many other unmaintained projects nobody cares about.

Any chance to open pydbus in a way, that enables a hypothetical 
pydbus_async module to coexist without monkey patching (or forking) pydbus?

Kind regards,
   Anselm

Indeed this is different from `wraps`. `wraps` is not a checker, but rather a way to encapsulate non-pint aware code. That is why it takes quantities, pass their (converted) magnitude into the function and then use the return value as the magnitude of a new quantity.  Your line of reasoning  make sense. Hey Sebastian, a couple questions/updates.

A) on `check_Xy_ndarray_or_df`, - I ended up just adding an optional `allow_DF` param to `check_Xy` which defaults to False. So everywhere that calls `check_Xy` still enforces the ndarray requirement for `X` (by default), except in `plot_decision_regions` which will run `check_Xy` with `allow_DF=True`. 

Hopefully that makes sense to you?  I didn't have to change much in `check_Xy` and added a couple new test cases which work as expected. But if you don't like the changes to `check_Xy` when you see the MR, I don't mind swapping it out for your original rec to create a new function.

B) (bigger question) - It seems to me that there's broadly two approaches I might take for the general changes to `plot_decision_regions` to make it compatible with getting a numpy array or pandas DF for `X`:

1. if passed `X` as a DF, convert it to a numpy array up top, and assign the ndarray column-indices of the original DF column names to `feature_index`. Then all the ndarray subscripting downstream works as is. Think I would do the same with filler_feature_values (e.g. converting from a dict with column-names as keys to a dict with ndarray col indices as keys).
 
2. Change all the lines with subscripting code like `X[...` to something like `slice_ndarray_or_df(X, row_nums, col_index_or_col_name)`. 

At first I thought your "helper function" would do something like number 2, but now I'm thinking you meant use it as in approach 1. above, right?

I think I (strongly?) prefer option 1., even though in the scope of this ticket, `Z = clf.predict(X_predict...` would still have to be called on the X_predict reconstructed as a DF (since we're presuming the `clf` has been fitted on a DF). And it sorta has to be re-constructed as a DF, not just on the preserved DF passed in, because `.predict` happens downstream of the `filler_values` getting dropped into X_predict.

Processors are CPU bound, it would not help running more than number of cores + 1~2 (or 10%~20% if you have more than 20 cores) instance. It may cost a lot CPU resources on thread switching(otherwise you are running slow database connections in processors).

Because [GIL](https://wiki.python.org/moin/GlobalInterpreterLock), running multiple threads will not incread performance on CPU bound components.

To learn the best instance number, you can refer to the queue status on dashboard. Only increase the number of instance of downstream of choked queue.

Hi. Due to this issue with backintime in archlinux (bit-team/backintime#545) I can confirm that the patch proposed by Germar in the according Archlinux bug (https://bugs.archlinux.org/task/48380) works (I adapted it to be applied to the sourcecode directly):

```patch
--- a/keyring/backends/kwallet.py	2016-02-04 16:56:03.000000000 +0100
+++ b/keyring/backends/kwallet.py	2016-03-02 10:31:09.234734254 +0100
@@ -24,9 +24,9 @@
         except dbus.DBusException as exc:
             raise RuntimeError(exc.get_dbus_message())
         try:
-            bus.get_object('org.kde.kwalletd', '/modules/kwalletd')
+            bus.get_object('org.kde.kwalletd5', '/modules/kwalletd5')
         except dbus.DBusException:
-            raise RuntimeError('cannot connect to org.kde.kwalletd')
+            raise RuntimeError('cannot connect to org.kde.kwalletd5')
         return 5.1
 
     def __init__(self, *arg, **kw):
@@ -41,7 +41,7 @@
         self.folder = 'Python'
         self.appid = 'Python program'
         try:
-            remote_obj = bus.get_object('org.kde.kwalletd', '/modules/kwalletd')
+            remote_obj = bus.get_object('org.kde.kwalletd5', '/modules/kwalletd5')
             self.iface = dbus.Interface(remote_obj, 'org.kde.KWallet')
             self.handle = self.iface.open(
                         self.iface.networkWallet(), wId, self.appid)
```

List is unfortunately *invariant* as of PEP 484, which means that nothing is a subclass of ``List[Any]`` but ``List[Any]`` itself. Reason for this is that list is writable. I suppose ``Sequence[Any]`` would do what you want.

```
T = typing.TypeVar('T', covariant=True)
L = typing.List[T]
type_util._issubclass(L[float], L[typing.Any])
```
Using a covariant typevar as value for an invariant type parameter seems invalid to me. This is currently undefined behavior, but I suppose ``pytypes`` should throw an error in this case. That said, it seems to accept it, but you're right the observed behaviour is not very pleasant.
```
type_util._issubclass(L[float], L)
```
should behave like the ``Any`` version. So labelling this as a bug...

IPython runs `repr()` on job object created in `Scheduler.every()`, which accesses `self.job_func.args` (where `self.job_func` is `None` at this time) BEFORE it is set in `Job.do()`.

`repr()` on `Job` can be called only AFTER `Job.do()` initializes the `Job` instance.

You would just write a continuously running Python program that had a main loop in it that was always running (and probably with a builtin 5 minute sleep inside that main loop).

You would probably use something like `supervisord` to ensure that your program was always running and restart it if it failed.

Note, I probably wouldn't try to keep the connection to the device always alive; I would probably just login to the device every 5 minutes; do what you need to do; and then disconnect.

If you want to keep the connection to the device always alive, then you are going to need to add logic into your program and checks if the connection is alive or not. If the connection is not alive, then you will need to re-connect. You might also need to check that other things like firewalls and VTY timers don't tear down the connection automatically.

IB-insync is designed for single-threaded asynchronous use as an alternative to multi threading. Multi threading is not the recommended way to go. However it can be done if all the work takes place in the same thread and event loop:

```python
from ibapi.wrapper import EWrapper
from ib_insync import *

from threading import Thread
import asyncio


class TestClient(Client, EWrapper):

    def __init__(self):
        EWrapper.__init__(self)
        Client.__init__(self, self)

    def tickPrice(self, *args):
        print('tickPrice', *args)

    def tickSize(self, *args):
        print('tickSize', *args)


def work():
    loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

    client = TestClient()
    client.connect(host='127.0.0.1', port=7497, clientId=2)
    reqId = client.getReqId()
    client.reqMktData(reqId, Forex('EURUSD'), '', False, False, [])
    client.run()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    thread = Thread(target=work)
    thread.start()

```

@Kronuz I do not think there is ever a use case to override the behaviour of TRUE or FALSE?
And for sanity and for the same reasons True and False cannot be subclassed in Python I think it is cleanest to keep them this way.   
Here is what Guido says on this topic https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-dev/2002-March/020822.html
>> You can always implement the latter as a subtype of bool if you care
>> enough and without breaking code.

> I thought about this last night, and realized that you shouldn't be allowed to subclass bool at all!  A subclass would only be useful when it has instances, but the mere existance of an instance of a subclass of bool would break the invariant that True and False are the only instances of bool!  (An instance of a subclass of C is also an instance of C.)  I think it's important not to provide a backdoor to create additional bool instances, so I think bool should not be subclassable.

I implemented adding images to SlidePlaceholders by doing this:

```python

class SlidePlaceholder(_BaseSlidePlaceholder):
    """
    Placeholder shape on a slide. Inherits shape properties from its
    corresponding slide layout placeholder.
    """
    def insert_picture(self, image_file, crop=True):
        """
        Return a |PlaceholderPicture| object depicting the image in
        *image_file*, which may be either a path (string) or a file-like
        object. The image is cropped to fill the entire space of the
        placeholder. A |PlaceholderPicture| object has all the properties and
        methods of a |Picture| shape except that the value of its
        :attr:`~._BaseSlidePlaceholder.shape_type` property is
        `MSO_SHAPE_TYPE.PLACEHOLDER` instead of `MSO_SHAPE_TYPE.PICTURE`.
        """
        pic = self._new_placeholder_pic(image_file, crop) # pass new parameter "method"
        self._replace_placeholder_with(pic)
        return PlaceholderPicture(pic, self._parent)

    def _new_placeholder_pic(self, image_file, crop=True):
        """
        Return a new `p:pic` element depicting the image in *image_file*,
        suitable for use as a placeholder. In particular this means not
        having an `a:xfrm` element, allowing its extents to be inherited from
        its layout placeholder.
        """
        rId, desc, image_size = self._get_or_add_image(image_file)
        id_, name = self.shape_id, self.name

        # Cropping the image, as in the original file
        if crop:
            pic = CT_Picture.new_ph_pic(id_, name, desc, rId)
            pic.crop_to_fit(image_size, (self.width, self.height))

        # Adjusting image to placeholder size and replace placeholder.
        else:
            ph_w, ph_h = self.width, self.height
            aspectPh = ph_w / ph_h

            img_w, img_h = image_size
            aspectImg = img_w / img_h

            if aspectPh > aspectImg:
                w = int(ph_h * aspectImg)
                h = ph_h # keep the height
            else:
                w = ph_w # keep the width
                h = int(ph_w / aspectImg)

            top = self.top + (ph_h - h) / 2
            left = self.left + (ph_w - w) / 2

            pic = CT_Picture.new_pic(id_, name, desc, rId, self.left + (ph_w - w) / 2, self.top, w, h)
        return pic

    def _get_or_add_image(self, image_file):
        """
        Return an (rId, description, image_size) 3-tuple identifying the
        related image part containing *image_file* and describing the image.
        """
        image_part, rId = self.part.get_or_add_image_part(image_file)
        desc, image_size = image_part.desc, image_part._px_size
        return rId, desc, image_size
```

Hi I actually have never made a pull request so forgive me if this isn't really what I'm supposed to do with it, I just wanted to illustrate how my app engine branch works. Take it or reject it as you will.

It has been a few years since this was discussed, but as a developer using Google App Engine I use this python-twitter library and I wanted to quickly share how I got it to work for people like me who stumbled across this issue.

Paste the python-twitter module folder (the twitter folder inside, not the whole root of this repository) directly into your App Engine project folder (where you put app.yaml).

My main issue was the requests library, which Google now offers a "monkeypatch" so the library will run through their `urlfetch` function instead of using the standard requests.

My fix was to add these lines after the requests import to the top of `api.py`:
```
...
import requests
import requests_toolbelt.adapters.appengine
requests_toolbelt.adapters.appengine.monkeypatch()
...
```

Now all the requests use App Engine's urlfetch, which deals with the fact that your App Engine instance is behind a bunch of datacentre routing/firewalls and returns everything as you would expect.

I believe you also need to include SSL in your app.yaml:
```
libraries:
- name: ssl
  version: latest
```

And finally, I was getting an error with the caching but instead of figuring out how to get that to work with App Engine's caching, I manually disabled python-twitter caching by forcing `None` at the `SetCache` method again in `api.py`. Your use case might not need this part (I'm not an expert on caching strategies).

I don't know if it makes sense to reformat the python-twitter models into something that'll work more tightly with datastore, but for my usecase I just made new datastore models with the properties I wanted to keep from the content returned by the library's api.

So for this library, I would say a good App Engine branch todo list would look like this, with #1 being the only truly breaking issue to fix:

1. Either reformat the requests to use urlfetch directly, or add the monkeystrap described above.
2. Integrate App Engine's memcache.
3. Define the Twitter models as NDB datastore models.
Make sure to add tests, and extra classifier information to setup.py as well.

@mouse07410 @thethythy thanks for your replies. I was digging into this again, and while I understand what you are saying, and it is correct, I don't think code in this library is handling it correctly.

If you look here https://github.com/yann2192/pyelliptic/blob/b9e9ae5ae0884ca817d834e24606e690bb6d2820/pyelliptic/ecc.py#L515 the `pubkey` variable is included along with the ciphertext. That *is* the **sender's public key** which as you said is passed along with the encrypted message. I think the second code sample I sent in the original ticket here should still work since you have the **sender's public key** as well as the **recipient's private key** which should be all that is required to decrypt:

```python
# this raises exception
new_ecc = pyelliptic.ECC(privkey=private_key, curve='prime256v1')
result = new_ecc.decrypt(ciphertext)
```

--

Upon looking further at the code in this library, I think something else is looks fishy. With ECIES (from what I understand from reading about it) the shared secret (ECDH) should be derived from the **sender's private key** and **recipient's public key**, but in the code it looks like it is derived from two public keys. Take a look at https://github.com/yann2192/pyelliptic/blob/b9e9ae5ae0884ca817d834e24606e690bb6d2820/pyelliptic/ecc.py#L270.

I hope I am missing something here since I am not an encryption expert and reading code can be difficult, but when looking at other code examples in other languages for ECIES they usually create an ephemeral key before encrypting and use the private key from that along with the public key of the recipient. I do not see that happening here.

For example you can see in this go implementation https://github.com/obscuren/ecies/blob/7c0f4a9b18d992166452d8cd32caaefd92b26386/ecies.go#L227-L233 that a new key is generated `R` and on line 233 it computes the shared secret using that along with the recipient's public key.

Hopefully this is just a misunderstanding.

@DRMacIver I think, this strategy is ready for the first iteration now.
It implements:
- Filter by unicode categories
- Filter by code points range
- Filter by exact characters

I spent a lot of time trying to make shrinking reuse charstree structure instead of doing blind random generation. However, I end with the tradeoff when I can greatly optimize cases when some specific Unicode category or character of specific code point range causes failure, but it hurts generic cases like `find(text(), lambda x: x >= u'☃')` - I have to spend more time and rounds to shrink toward to `'☃'` value. So current Unicode shrinking is good enough and balanced well. Ascii one though improved a bit, but assuming code points range there it will not give a big difference.

However, the general problem had solved well and `characters` strategy is able to quickly produce chars for any code point, even quite rare cases.

P.S. You may be interested in: https://travis-ci.org/DRMacIver/hypothesis/jobs/92345632#L889

Hi @rmishra7 


> Equal (=) sign with string (=str) getting replaced with 0

That is in LibreCalc. In Excel this would be an error since there this expression looks like a formula but there is no defined name called `transformer`:


![aa_image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/94267/29658489-ec441a04-88b2-11e7-8d9b-c6db38129fc4.png)


Either way, that is the [documented behaviour ](http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/worksheet.html#worksheet-write)for the `write()` method. It treats strings starting with `=` as a formula:

Strings are then handled as follows:

>    Strings that start with "=" are take to match a formula and are written using write_formula(). This can be overridden, see below.
> ...
> The Workbook() constructor option takes three optional arguments that can be used to override string handling in the write() function. These options are shown below with their default values:

```python
xlsxwriter.Workbook(filename, {'strings_to_numbers':  False,
                               'strings_to_formulas': True,
                               'strings_to_urls':     True})
```

So if you wish to avoid this, then use `write_string()` instead of `write()` or use `'strings_to_formulas': False` in the constructor.

The transit-relay is now running on a new host, with usage database and munin data ported over. Everything appears to be stable. I'm still running the port forwarders from the old host, but they haven't seen any traffic for 24 hours.

https://github.com/warner/magic-wormhole-mailbox-server contains the first cut at the split-out rendezvous/mailbox server. I've tagged release 0.1.0, but there's still some work to do before it's ready for migration. The biggest item is a migration tool, to copy the usage data out of the old database into the new one. The new package uses two separate databases: one for the mandatory channel data (nameplates, mailboxes, messages), plus an optional one for historical usage data. The munin plugins feed from the usage data.

next steps:
* remove server code from magic-wormhole, leaving only client code
* rewrite magic-wormhole tests in terms of an imported magic-wormhole-mailbox-server package
* figure out future plans for ad-hoc networks (#48), which might require pulling the mailbox server back into the client (or having the client always depend upon magic-wormhole-mailbox-server. Maybe we could split out the core of the server into yet another package, and have that get used by both magic-wormhole (client, for the ad-hoc/multicast cast) and magic-wormhole-mailbox-server (for the central websocket-reachable case)
* migrate the mailbox server to a new host too

Umm, does this actually *need* `.asdict()` and `astuple()` given the existence of `vars()`? Since all values will just be in `__dict__` as normal, `vars()` should just work, as with normal classes. This might be fragile going forward, so maybe that's the reason?

```python
>>> class CCC:
	def __init__(self, a, b, c):
		self.a = a
		self.b = b
		self.c = c
		
>>> z = CCC(1, 2, 3)
>>> vars(z)
{'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
```

`namedtuple` needs `.asdict()` because it doesn't put things in `__dict__`:

```python
>>> from collections import namedtuple
>>> x = namedtuple("AAA", "a b c")(1, 2, 3)
>>> x
AAA(a=1, b=2, c=3)
>>> vars(x)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<pyshell#3>", line 1, in <module>
    vars(x)
TypeError: vars() argument must have __dict__ attribute
```

Classes which set default values as class attributes and don't write them onto an instance also suffer:
```python
>>> class BBB:
	a = 1
	b = 2
	c = 3

	
>>> y = BBB()
>>> vars(y)
{}
>>> y.__dict__
{}
```

As far as the attrs API, this is taken from their documentation at http://www.attrs.org/en/stable/api.html#attr.fields. An `attr.Attribute` is the logical equivalent of what I'm calling a `Field`.
```
>>> @attr.s
... class C(object):
...     x = attr.ib()
...     y = attr.ib()
>>> attr.fields(C)
(Attribute(name='x', default=NOTHING, validator=None, repr=True, cmp=True, hash=None, init=True, convert=None, metadata=mappingproxy({})), Attribute(name='y', default=NOTHING, validator=None, repr=True, cmp=True, hash=None, init=True, convert=None, metadata=mappingproxy({})))
>>> attr.fields(C)[1]
Attribute(name='y', default=NOTHING, validator=None, repr=True, cmp=True, hash=None, init=True, convert=None, metadata=mappingproxy({}))
>>> attr.fields(C).y is attr.fields(C)[1]
True
```
So, the `Attribute` for `y` can be accessed as `.y` or `[1]`.

I think using an OrderedDict is reasonable. I already build this data structure, but I just keep the `values()`.

This special-casing seems like adding complexity to our code and potentially confusing to the users.

IME we need a way to define factories anyway so there’s that.  I agree that a core API of the stdlib should be as unsurprising as possible so my idea special case (in accordance with explicit > implicit) would be to raise a helpful error, if e.g. `[]` is passed instead of silently copying it.

In the best case that might educate the user on the perils of mutable default arguments.

Rather a link (rst ref) is created referencing the $ref target.

Replace
```
Our Schema
^^^^^^^^^^


.. jsonschema:: base.yml
```

by


```
Our Schema
^^^^^^^^^^


.. jsonschema:: base.yml

.. jsonschema:: https://github.com/tardis-sn/tardis/tree/master/tardis/io/schemas/supernova.yml
```

There are a few options:

1. Use the django admin LogEntry module, which stores the same information,
but only for models in the admin system.
2. Create your own signal handlers and you own models that store this
information in parallel with the revisions being created.

The reason these fields were removed from django-reversion was that they
were only useful for generating statistics about user activity on the site,
and were not used internally by django-reversion. As such, they are a prime
candidate for writing as a separate set of functionality.

If you decide not to change the expiration time, you might want to add a note in the documentation mentioning that it's one year for backwards-compatibility reasons, and suggest that users set it explicitly.

Regarding the manifest backend, you probably don't even need the regex. Looking at [whitenoise's implementation](https://github.com/evansd/whitenoise/blob/bf292167eed07d901a2649ba97aaf11f117ee6ab/whitenoise/middleware.py#L107), they just see if there's any suffix separated by periods, and if there is they redo the versioning and compare the filenames. Or you could do the stricter regex to avoid doing some needless work (at the cost of being more closely coupled to Django's naming convention). Either way should work. 

If you want to keep the logic in the regular file backend you'll probably have to redo the hash. That's not a big deal, but wouldn't you have to fetch the original file back from S3? The Storage API just passes you the bytes on a file-by-file basis, right, you have no way to access an arbitrary static file on disk? That wouldn't be ideal. If that's true it would probably be better to modify the manifest backend and access its global mapping of hashes. (Or I suppose you could create your own global mapping in the file backend for this purpose.)

I think `urlparse`'s interface and goals are a little different than `tldextract`'s, namely in `urlparse`'s strict input and standards validation. `tldextract` meanwhile focuses only on TLDs and is loose with them. It's also a simple implementation. I'd rather not cross it and confuse it with an orthogonal lib.

For example, here's a difference in interface I'd rather not have to explain :)

>>> import tldextract, urlparse
>>> tldextract.extract('google.com')
ExtractResult(subdomain='', domain='google', tld='com')
>>> urlparse.urlsplit('google.com')
SplitResult(scheme='', netloc='', path='google.com', query='', fragment='')

I would start it with JSON for to be  more forward thinking, [as mentioned](https://github.com/john-kurkowski/tldextract/issues/81#issuecomment-193245567). 

As a concrete use case, metadata about each suffix will be required for #66, i.e. whether it's a private suffix or not. Teasing this from JSON is easy. Teasing from plain text is less fun.

There's no reason why it should work. ``in`` in Python is used to check if an item is available in an iterable data structure and a resource object isn't that kind of data structure by design. You need to use ``hasattr`` or ``getattr`` with default value, e.g.:

```python
i=rm.issue.get(1)

if hasattr(i, 'notes'):
    # do something

print getattr(i, 'notes', 'no notes, sorry') // 3rd arg is the default value is notes attr isn't avalable
```

These are 2 pythonic ways to work with attributes that may not always be available.

@bbenne10 @mcs07 the issue with setting `self._state = state` is that `security.init_app` is designed to be used with the [Flask factory pattern](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.10/patterns/appfactories/), and an instance of `security` needs to be able to work with multiple instances of a Flask `app`. `app.extensions['security']` returns the `state` which is associated with the specific instance of `app`, which is what allows my above solution to work.

My working theory is that since pyodbc is expecting that SQL_GUID is a string, it will convert whatever is passed in to a string. So, since the string version of a UUID object includes the dashes (which is why Django strips them out), the format should line up and it should "just work".

I was able to test this out by enabling the `has_native_uuid_field` feature temporarily. It appears as though the compilers are generating the right format for a UUID, so that's promising.

```python
>>> from sql_server.pyodbc.features import DatabaseFeatures
>>> DatabaseFeatures.has_native_uuid_field = True
>>> import uuid
>>> test_uuid = uuid.uuid4()
>>> from foo.models import Bar
>>> bars = Bar.objects.filter(id=test_uuid)
>>> str(bars.query)
'SELECT [foo_bar].[id] FROM [foo_bar] WHERE [foo_bar].[id] = 3d6dba9e-5313-419f-914c-33117e7f470c'
```

When it actually hits pyodbc as a prepared statement though... [pyodbc complains](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/blob/f94251a5b62252fff0e7057b6f70260cb4bf4fb0/src/params.cpp#L578), because it doesn't convert it to a string by default. So, back to the drawing board.

**Edit:** When I forced all UUID params to be strings, it did in fact generate a valid query. But I have not yet found a clean hook point where that can be done.

I would not recommend using `retry` or any kind of retrying behavior on bitcoind RPC requests. Connection timeout can happen for many reasons, even at the "end" of an RPC call. Retrying the same RPC call could cause buggy behavior such as "sending money twice". Bitcoin RPC protocol is not designed to prevent replays.

https://botbot.me/freenode/bitcoin-core-dev/2016-11-21/?msg=76819688&page=2

Hello,

Thanks for opening a ticket here. Before going any further, I believe those things could be a problem:

* The serializer used for translations must be in `Meta` under name `translations_serialize`, not `translations`. Otherwise it won't be used.
* It must be a serializer class, not an instance. For instance, `ProductTranslationSerializer`.
* It is very recommended that the class used as `serializer_class` inherits `hvad.contrib.restframework.NestedTranslationSerializer`, otherwise some field types may raise odd errors. If they don't, you're fine but it's better to use it and avoid the issue entirely.


Now, one of the main concepts of hvad is an object always has to be complete. That means at no point can an object not have any translation (and if such an object is created, it is an error and the behavior is unspecified).
Here, you are using the `PUT` http verb, which implies the data being sent is a full representation of the object. Therefore it must include at least one translation, or it will break the complete-object rule.

Now if you really insist on doing that, the way you wrote it should work once the items above are fixed. That is, your serializer will override the one provided by `TranslationsMixin`, causing it to no longer be required and forcing `TranslationsMixin` to disable itself.
Be aware that this throws away hvad's checks and you'll have to be very careful not to let your api create orphan models with no translation.

That gives us a simple way to get code to execute inside the virtualenv, and it also lets us dodge the problem of how to convince sphinx-build to load our code. Hi @wexi . That issue might happen for a number of scenarios:

- there's another, older 'backports' package installed and taking ownership of the namespace
- (as webknjaz says) if you don't have the package properly installed in the same environment where you're trying to use it.
- the namespace package wasn't installed properly
- there's another backports directory masking the namespace

But it works for me:

```
~ $ python2.7 -m pip install backports.functools_lru_cache
Collecting backports.functools_lru_cache
  Using cached https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/03/8e/2424c0e65c4a066e28f539364deee49b6451f8fcd4f718fefa50cc3dcf48/backports.functools_lru_cache-1.5-py2.py3-none-any.whl
Installing collected packages: backports.functools-lru-cache
Successfully installed backports.functools-lru-cache-1.5
~ $ python2.7 -c "from backports.functools_lru_cache import lru_cache" && echo "worked!"
worked!
```

You can start to troubleshoot by running `pip list | grep backports` and see if maybe there are other old backports installed and upgrade or uninstall it.

If that doesn't help, try uninstalling all backports packages and see what's claiming the backports package name with `python -c "import backports; print(backports.__file__)"`. Trace that file and maybe delete whatever is supplying it.

If that doesn't get you straight, try replicating your issue on a clean Linux host. Maybe build a Docker image that replicates the issue. If you can provide steps to replicate the issue, we can almost certainly help resolve the issue.

Good luck. I'm going to close this ticket as there's unlikely a bug with the project, but feel free to respond back with more details.

The problem is probably not with `u` or with the SDK, but with the client. One or more of these things is probably the case:
- The application is not configured to support the `As-User` feature (it lacks the necessary scope).
- The authenticated user does not have permissions to use the `As-User` feature (it isn't an admin or a service account).
- The authenticated user does not have permissions to impersonate user `u`.

For the first issue, see https://developer.box.com/docs/configuring-box-platform#section-3-enabling-app-auth-and-app-users and https://developer.box.com/docs/configuring-service-accounts#section-5-select-advanced-features .

Hello @ChrisJohannnesson, the warning that you reported is thrown in case the loaded swagger spec contains un-standard formats that are not handled via [User Defined Fromat](https://bravado-core.readthedocs.io/en/stable/formats.html).
Defining the UDF should remove your warning, but in general UDF are meant to add validation and conversions utilities for speicified types.

If you need additional support please provide a small snippet of the swagger spec and the python code, so it will be easier to help you.

---
UDF are useful to convert JSON data to a more manageble and easier to use python objects.
An example could be `format: date` for `type: string`.
A valid json representation of this type of object could be `"2018-07-27"`, thanks to the [defined UDF](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/v5.0.4/bravado_core/formatter.py#L112), it will be converted to `datetime.date(2018,7,27)`

A valid RFC 3339 format needs to have the timezone information. The timezone could be provided by hour offset (``-08:00`` in case of PST time) or by a letter (such as ``Z`` for ``UTC``).

To have the correct behaviour is important to have a localized datetime object (ie. ``pytz.timezone('Los_Angeles').localize(<näive datetime object>)``).
```python
maci $ ipython
Python 2.7.12 (default, Oct 11 2016, 05:20:59)
Type "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

IPython 5.1.0 -- An enhanced Interactive Python.
?         -> Introduction and overview of IPython's features.
%quickref -> Quick reference.
help      -> Python's own help system.
object?   -> Details about 'object', use 'object??' for extra details.

In [1]: import strict_rfc3339

In [2]: import datetime

In [3]: import pytz

In [4]: strict_rfc3339.validate_rfc3339(datetime.datetime.now().isoformat())
Out[4]: False

In [5]: strict_rfc3339.validate_rfc3339(pytz.utc.localize(datetime.datetime.now()).isoformat())
Out[5]: True
```

I think it's important that the user be able to set the signal handling, which this seems to prevent. This also makes it impossible for a process group with ProcessDevices to disable interrupts, because the subprocesses inherit signals sent to the parent, so sending SIGINT to a process which has explicitly disabled SIGINT will stay up, but all of its devices will shut down.

I think following the standard behavior of inheriting the parent process environment makes the most sense, and cases where the context should differ can do so, as you've shown here.

Alternately, restoring the default handlers could make sense, but if we do so we must also detach it from the current process group, so that it doesn't receive its parent's signal handlers.

zmq sockets are not threadsafe, so we cannot share this socket across threads, and there are one or more IO threads per context. We would need to find a way to ensure that the socket is unique per IO thread. This is knowable per-context for the common case of `io_threads=1`, but not in general, so selecting which socket to use has to happen in the `free_fn` itself. I've wanted to do this, but need a simple way to get a thread identity that works on posix and Windows.

class types.SimpleNamespace
A simple object subclass that provides attribute access to its namespace, as well as a meaningful repr.

class SimpleNamespace:
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        self.__dict__.update(kwargs)

    def __repr__(self):
        keys = sorted(self.__dict__)
        items = ("{}={!r}".format(k, self.__dict__[k]) for k in keys)
        return "{}({})".format(type(self).__name__, ", ".join(items))

    def __eq__(self, other):

New in version 3.3.

At http://splinter.readthedocs.org/en/latest/drivers/phantomjs.html I read:

"PhantomJS is a headless WebKit scriptable with a JavaScript API, to use 
the driver first you must install <http://phantomjs.org/download.html> 
it in your machine. The Phantomjs WebDriver is provided by Selenium 2.0, 
thus you need to install Selenium 2.0 via pip"

After much head-scratching and staring at that paragraph I /finally 
/noticed that the word "install" is a link to phantomjs.org.

So indeed the docs are correct /in that part/. Given that it was hard 
for me to see that link I recommend it be made more visible or evident. 
Also the next sentence led me to believe that selenium provided 
phantomjs, so I did not even look for the download link.

Note:
   selenium does /not /provide the phantomjs webdriver.
/webkit /refers to certain browsers. Using webkit here is incorrect.
   phantomjs.exe must be in the executable path in order for subprocess 
to find it.
   as an aside, pip install splinter also installs selenuim.

Suggested revision:

"PhantomJS is a headless scriptable browser with a JavaScript API. To 
use PhantomJS you must install it in your machine. Go to 
http://phantomjs.org/download.html and follow the instructions.

After downloading and unzipping place phantomjs.exe in a folder that is 
included in PATH.

There may be better terminology for PATH in Linux et. al.

The upload API currently requires 4 steps to completion

1. get credentials
2. s3 cp the data
3. initiate import 
4. poll for completion status

How much abstraction do we want here? The Javascript sdk has the user write their own s3 upload code as a callback. https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-js/blob/master/lib/services/uploads.js#L77

This would certainly be an option for the python sdk too. But your description hints at doing a few of the steps (1, 2 and possibly 3) via a single, synchronous, high-level API call. @sgillies what are your thoughts on this?

.. Then of course we have list and delete but those seem a bit easier as they are quick, synchronous, single steps.

I'm not a fan of coercing strs to ints behind the scenes for Django model fields.
If you're dealing with GET requests, use a Django form with the provided an `IndexEnumField` which will convert the string to an int for you.
https://github.com/hearsaycorp/django-richenum/blob/master/src/django_richenum/forms/fields.py#L80
You should not be passing in unvalidated HTTP request data directly to your Django model.

You can try passing the parsed JSON string (presumably, it becomes a Python dict) to a Django form as well, which should bind the form with values.

If the JSON structure you're parsing is too complicated to represent with a Django form, try using normalize to parse the str as an int and then convert the int to a RichEnum.
https://github.com/hearsaycorp/normalize

Pykafka aims to remain completely ignorant of the contents of the messages it processes, so I don't think mechanisms allowing grouping based on message content will ever be added. That said, Kafka itself provides partition keying as a way to consume messages in meaningful subgroups within a topic. You can pass an instance of the [`HashingPartitioner`](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/b1bf00778d3f6458d99ddc14bb5ebf9bbeee9fab/pykafka/partitioners.py#L51) to the producer's `partitioner` kwarg. This will enable you to use the `partition_key` kwarg on `Producer.produce` to group messages logically (perhaps by some piece of their contents) into partitions on your topic. When consuming, you can use the `partitions` argument on `SimpleConsumer` to specify the exact partitions the consumer should listen to. This will allow each consumer to consume only messages from the same logical grouping.

I would also recommend examining your topic setup and considering using more topics to achieve your logical grouping. If every message in your topic is part of the same logical group, you'll be able to take advantage of pykafka's automatic consumption balancing via the `BalancedConsumer` class while maintaining your application's particular logical grouping of messages.

`requests.request` already uses a [session under the hood](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requests/api.py#L55-L56) for a single call. The perk of having your client aware of the session is if you wanted to manually manage the session for multiple calls. As is, the session would not get closed after being used. Which might be nice if you're making several API calls in the same process, but it also means those connections would stay open even if a call is made early in a process and never again. 
Also you'd want the session in the `RequestsClient.__init__`  rather than the base `HTTPClient` because this will error if the user was doesn't have `requests` installed.

@hohlraum I apologize for letting this fall through the cracks. You bring up some great points for consideration.

My feeling is that our default behavior should be along the lines of case 1 - no filtering. Allow the user to filter if they choose to in an advanced workflow using `hough_line` and operating in the Hough accumulator space directly.

The problem with trying to merge lines, even close ones, is in the way the parameter space is normalized. We use a parameterization where one axis is angle, and the other is the shortest distance from the origin to any point on the line. In this parameter space, there is no easy or efficient way to identify similarly-sloped lines. Lines 1 and 2 look very different in r/theta space, because while theta is identical the r value is not. We have to use this space because it's bounded.

If we did choose to add this functionality, I would be fine with patching it in via keyword arguments as you suggest. Thus, if a solution presents itself in the future we have a path forward. Ergo, I recommend we move forward with this simpler PR as it's blocking a couple others and does fix a known problem. Thoughts?

What do you think about supporting multiple importable global flags, either as a replacement for or in addition to `settings.FLAGS`? I'm thinking individual apps might want to define their own flags, so you could do something like:

 ```py
 # settings.py
 FLAGS = {
     'foo': True,
     'bar': False,
     ...
 }
 
 FLAG_SOURCES = (
     'knowledgebase.flags',
     'v1.flags',
     ...
 )
 ``` 

 This is similar to how Django allows for import of template processors, middleware, etc. from named modules. It would also allow individual apps to define and manage their own app state without requiring a commit to the general `settings.py`.

Managing these efficiently might mean some kind of cached global set of flags which is generated once on first settings load (maybe during this app's `AppConfig.ready`?) and can then be efficiently iterated over in future. Potential complication would be how to handle multiply-defined flags.

Listen, let's try to be a bit practical here. Here's what happened:

- inveniosoftware/invenio-records-ui#63 changed API and this change wasn't advertised in the PR, and I didn't detect it when I reviewed and merged the PR.
- We've had zenodo/zenodo#881 hanging around for quite a while which are fixing various user facing problems, and have been blocked on it due to various other Invenio PRs on deposit, accounts, records-rest, files-rest, records-files. Finally last blocker inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit#143 was merged and released and we could deploy today. 
- We deployed on first sandbox then production, but too late detected that *all* previews were broken due to the API change in Records-UI.
- We had scheduled a strategy meeting for the entire day, and thus urgently need to fix the issue. We could have fixed the it directly in Zenodo with a wrapper function, instead we choose for the benefit of everyone else to fix Invenio-Preview and get a fast release.
- The tag was already pushed (but master not due to missing LGTM) when @jirikuncar  made his comment and we were already in the meeting, hence short message and no time to fix.

So, it's an unfortunate chain of events that led to this, but let's try to be a bit practical. We're talking about a couple of version bumps in a release commit. We got a problem fixed for the benefit of all of Invenio, when we could just have done it by ourselves and let the rest of you waste the time in fixing it. I think you are making it really hard to be motivated about contributing to Invenio if tiny mistakes like this are blown out of proportion when we in fact were thinking about the Invenio community and being unselfish. 

So, yes, release commits should not include other stuff but it's a tiny mistake. On the other hand I think pushing away Invenio contributors is far my harmful and with long-term devastating effect to Invenio than a couple of version bumps in a release commit.

I've looked at this a little. `__annotations__` was introduced [way back in PEP-3107](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3107/#accessing-function-annotations) for Python 3. Functions and methods grew the possibility to have an `__annotations__` attribute:
```pycon
Python 3.4.8 (default, Mar 29 2018, 16:18:25)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 9.0.0 (clang-900.0.39.2)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> class Foo:
...     def thing(a:int) -> int:
...         return 1
...
>>> Foo.thing.__annotations__
{'a': <class 'int'>, 'return': <class 'int'>}
```

Python 3.6 accepted [PEP-563](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0526) which let you put annotations on raw variables. Prior to that, only function/method arguments and return types could be annotated AIUI, and I can't find a way to get `__annotations__` on a class. But with 3.6, you can now have `__annotations__` showing up at class scope:
```pycon
Python 3.6.2 (v3.6.2:5fd33b5926, Jul 16 2017, 20:11:06)
[GCC 4.2.1 (Apple Inc. build 5666) (dot 3)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> class Foo:
...     a:int
...
>>> Foo.__annotations__
{'a': <class 'int'>}
```

PEP 563 only changes the values stored in the annotations dictionary into strings always. It's not enabled by default in 3.7 and requires a `__future__` import:

```pycon
Python 3.7.0rc1 (default, Jun 13 2018, 16:20:06)
[Clang 9.1.0 (clang-902.0.39.2)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from __future__ import annotations
>>> class Foo:
...   a:int
...
>>> Foo.__annotations__
{'a': 'int'}
```

z.a.attribute.AttributeAnnotations uses `__annotations__` and can thus conflict in Python 3.6 and above, leading to false sharing (and failure to persist):
```pycon
>>> from zope.annotation import attribute
>>> foo = Foo()
>>> attr_ann = AttributeAnnotations(foo)
>>> len(attr_ann)
1
>>> attr_ann['baz'] = 'bar'
>>> foo.__annotations__
{'a': 'int', 'baz': 'bar'}
>>> Foo.__annotations__
{'a': 'int', 'baz': 'bar'}
```

I'm not sure how to avoid it. The obvious solution is to use `vars` directly instead of `__getitem__`, but that breaks in the presence of `__slots__`, or more generally when a descriptor is used.

Preface to my commentary: All I truly "need" from an upstream library is a stable API for me and a stable ABI for end users to link against. Choosing your own cost/benefit tradeoffs on how to implement that is, in the end, up to you; I'm just some guy on the Internet with an opinion, and I'll likely disappear after this issue. :smile: That said, I'll respond to @nemequ's last bit and then let you all duke out how you want to proceed...

> I'm not really a C++ person, but IIRC they have a bool type which is (can be or must be, not sure which) implemented with an enum.

`bool` is a built-in type in C++; there's no enum "implementation" that I'm aware of.

> to get around it you can use _Bool (which is built-in, doesn't require a header)

There is no `_Bool` type in C++. And including stdbool.h in C++ is not even guaranteed to provide a `_Bool` macro (it does in GCC, but as I understand it, that's an extension).

I want to reiterate that

1. It's not just size you necessarily have to worry about. You want to make sure that the assembled code that deals with both types are equivalent, or you can bet that there will eventually be some bizarre corner case because of the mismatch.
2. I don't know if `bool`-incompatibility between the two languages is a problem anymore. Modern compiler ABIs may have defined the two equivalently for the platforms you're targeting; I don't know.

> Another option would be to use the build system to determine the value of sizeof(_Bool) and write an appropriate type, but that would require running an executable

If you're seriously considering this, I just have to know: what is so hate-worthy in the `int`-as-boolean idiom? :smile:

Anyway. I think I've said my piece and then some. As long as downstream doesn't break in weird corner cases, I'm happy.

These days, even MSVC supports stdbool.h (as of VS 2013).  I'd prefer for Brotli to simply use the system bool everywhere and just require C99 (or at least the subset of it supported by VS 2013).  Being stuck with C89 in 2016 is ridiculous.

C99 is pretty clear about boolean values; see § 7.16.  `true` is defined as 1, `false` is defined as 0.  Brotli defines `BROTLI_FALSE` as an enum value of 0 and `BROTLI_TRUE` as an enum value of `!BROTLI_FALSE` (which evaluates to 1), so the actual values shouldn't be an issue.  That said, I'd probably change `BROTLI_TRUE` to just be 1 instead of `!BROTLI_FALSE`.

The `_Bool` type, OTOH, could be a problem.  C99 specifies it as a type large enough to hold 0 or 1 (§ 6.2.5,2), so 1-byte is sufficient and that's what every implementation I'm aware of uses.  However, most compilers use `sizeof(int)` for enums, and Brotli defines `BROTLI_BOOL` as an enum when in C89 mode.  I'm guessing that's why you had a problem.

> I agree with the idea that a boolean concept is more documentation-friendly than int, but the current implementation causes problems.

Agreed, implementing it as an enum is a bad idea.

> (And I humbly submit that int is still the most "correct", idiomatic type for booleans in a portable C ABI.)

If you define "portable" as "C89+", then you're right.  However, targeting C99 is portable, and `bool` is the idiomatic type for C99, which pretty much every non-toy compiler other than MSVC has supported for a *very* long time.  Realistically, the only place `_Bool`/stdbool.h isn't going to be supported is people using old versions of MSVC.  Everyone else supports them even in C89 mode.

`sizeof(_Bool)` on Windows is 1, so I would go with something like:

```c
#if define(BROTLI_NO_STDBOOL) || (defined(_MSC_VER) && (_MSC_VER < 1800))
#  if !defined(bool)
#    define bool unsigned char
#  endif
#  if !defined(true)
#    define true 1
#  endif
#  if !defined(false)
#    define false 0
#  endif
#else
#include <stdbool.h>
#endif
```

Then you can get rid of all the `BROTLI_BOOL` cruft.  Virtually every compiler/platform should work as expected.  I'm sure there is some weird compiler somewhere which targets an sbscure DSP or something which won't work, but worst-case scenario people can explicitly define a few preprocesor symbols before including brotli.

Of course, if you're really attached to the `BROTLI_BOOL` idea, it would be easy enough to just `#define BROTLI_BOOL unsigned char` instead of using an enum.  `sizeof(_Bool)` is 1 on Windows and Linux x86_64, and probably most other places, though I haven't checked.

Thank you for using SentencePiece.

Though it is not documented, `spm_train --input=tsv_file --input_format=tsv ..`  allows for loading a corpus in tsv format where the first column is the sentence and the second column is frequency.  `--input_format=tsv` is originally prepared to train the model from the word list with frequency.

As for the training data, I feel that the default size 100M is enough for SPM training as long as they are sampled appropriately from the full corpus.  SentencePiece adopts a simple uni-gram language model with relatively smaller vocab size.  We would be able to get a reliable statistics only from the small subset of the corpus.  However, it would be nicer to check the output segmentation changing the size of corpus.

Some more investigations while we wait to hear what Aaron thinks of these images:

Building intuition on the effect of strut configurations
---------------------------------------------

In talking with Josh I realized I don't have a great intuition even for what happens if you have naive rectangular struts (i.e. not a proper spider) and you change the width, and that maybe learning about this would help me understand the results with struts vs. spider.  So I made the version with filled in struts of widths varying from 2% to 14% of the pupil diameter.  The one with narrow struts gives a PSF that looks like this:
![strut_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454906/20114367/006851a0-a5c2-11e6-8bda-309f6073546e.png)
The diffraction spikes are sort of approaching an unbroken line.  With wide struts we get this:
![strut_4](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454906/20114380/0e51cab2-a5c2-11e6-9a58-2cdc5b844a6b.png)
This has the bump-like features in the diffraction spikes, and the feature we saw above with struts that there is some modification of the obscured Airy features around the diffraction spikes.

So I think this kind of makes the above plots make more sense.

For getting the spider right, I found that Aaron did send me the details needed to do the treatment I had proposed:

> So I'm thinking that these could actually be handled directly using kwargs (instead of 
> by loading a pupil plane image, which is more expensive). The way to do this would be to 
> use the current code with 4 equally-spaced radial struts, but allow for the inner ~90% of 
> the struts to be missing and hence that area is unobscured.

For reference, the info he sent was as follows:

"The spiders are each 50mm wide and the distance between the spiders is 80cm — that is the distance from the midline of one to the midline of the other along the 45deg line.

Also note that the central obscuration is 61%, that is a radius of 2.55 meters compared to the primary’s radius of 4.18 meters (thats clear area, so little under the 4.2 m glass radius)"

So in principle, I could make it possible to just remove the inner X% of each strut (i.e. make that area unobscured) using the figures given by Aaron.  Or use a high-res FITS file, which has a bit of work needed to make compliant with our format, but not much.

Anybody have any thoughts on these options?  My only concern with the former is that since the needed resolution is calculated based on the aperture + obscuration ignoring the demands of representing struts, and the spider is very narrow, the first option will require careful manual tuning of the oversampling to make sure we're getting it right.

@ThomasGreiner Thanks for the clarification.

But FWIW, I disagree slightly that ABP is completely neutral on this issue.

This blog post:
https://adblockplus.org/blog/about-that-facebook-tracking-thing
and this page:
https://adblockplus.org/en/features#socialmedia

Contain /descriptions/ of what fanboy-social will do for you. Those descriptions focus on sharing buttons that track you even when you don't engage with them. They /don't/ make it clear that e.g. social LOGIN buttons will be effected.

(yes, whole login buttons disappear: https://github.com/lipis/bootstrap-social/issues/50)

I'd suggest ABP ought to adjust its /descriptions/ of fanboy-social to be more clear. OR, if they'd rather remain neutral, to stop endorsing it at all.

As things stand, I claim that from the perspective of an average ABP user who reads one of those pages, agrees that she doesn't like being tracked by Facebook share buttons she doesn't engage with, and installs the filter...it's a BUG that icons and indeed /whole login buttons/ suddenly disappear. I know anecdotally I've had a few users who were confused and frustrated that in some browsers they couldn't log in to my website.

(I've also emailed the fanboy-social maintainer at easylist.subscription@gmail.com directly. If people agree this is a bug in fanboy-social, they might consider doing the same.)

I don't intend to point fingers here. There are probably people who /want/ to block social login buttons, and it makes sense to me for ABP, with some customization, to provide that functionality. I think we just need to tighten up the the messaging so users don't get unexpected behavior and these projects don't have to play cat and mouse :)

IMO the first step there is drawing some distinctions in what /kinds/ of social things you might want to block. My first guess at that is something like (1) only things that track you without you even clicking (2) all share buttons ('I never want to share so don't even ask me'), but not login buttons (3) anything having anything to do with social, including sharing and logging in.
Having built around 50 sites using MODX I'd love to see this implemented. I see many use cases for the icon such as credit, login, reference and more. +1

@jasonrhaas I think I found the issue. It is mainly related to error handling. I was able to fix it using the ``FlaskApp.common_error_handler``

Here is a working applications with a normal Flask app and Connexion Api blueprint:

```python
from flask import Flask
from connexion import Api, FlaskApp


class Swagger:
    def blueprint(spec, **yaml_kwargs):
        api = Api(spec, swagger_ui=False, strict_validation=True,
                  validate_responses=False, arguments=yaml_kwargs)
        bp = api.blueprint

        return bp


def query():
    return {'success': True}


if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = Flask(__name__)
    bp = Swagger.blueprint('swagger.yaml')

    app.register_blueprint(bp)
    app.register_error_handler(Exception, FlaskApp.common_error_handler)

    app.run(port=9090, debug=True)

```
``query`` is the configured endpoint.

Are you running 4.1.x here? This seems to be the issue in pre-4.2 versions where the resource associated with a data object is the root resource in the hierarchy (causes irods/irods#3419, irods/irods#3503, etc.).

The `get` operation here runs a query to fetch all information in the catalog for the specified data object. The root resource of the hierarchy is stored in the `resource_name` column in 4.1.

If you print out the `resc_hier` instead, you can see the resource which actually stores the data object (and could use some python magic to just show the leaf resc if you want). Note that neither `resc_hier` nor `resource_name` are stored in the catalog in 4.2, so this is a 4.1-only problem/solution.

> When I update a variable which has no formula, it keeps using those old formulas !

I think it's not a bug, but a feature 😅. `update_variable` makes a lot of effort so that all the attributes of the variables being updated are kept. They are only modified if overridden in the new variable, including formulas.

I think the point of this behavior (which has been around for longer than me) is to avoid repeating attributes when defining a reform, and to allow to change only the most recents formulas.

Creating a `replace_variable`  methods sounds good to me.

> Donc si pas d'impact, préciser Impact : aucun. Ce sera clair.

Cette information est déjà transmise par le numéro de version, par le biais de [semver](http://semver.org) :

- S’il s’agit d’un patch, il n’y a aucun impact sur l’API publique.
- S’il s’agit d’un changement de numéro de version mineur, il peut y avoir un ajout dans l’API, mais aucune perte de compatibilité avec l’existant.
- Seul un changement de numéro de version majeur peut signaler un impact avec perte de rétrocompatibilité.

Il est important de détailler l’impact quand il existe. Mais définir « Impact : aucun » me semble ambigu : il va être difficile d’indiquer qu’un changement n’a « aucun impact »… tout dépend de l’usage visé. Et si l’impact est nul sur l’API publique, alors cette information est redondante avec le numéro de version.

Yes the `autosummary:: ` blocks need to be added manually.  They are directives to [Sphinx's autosummary extension](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/ext/autosummary.html) which produces tables of functions, methods and classes in the documentation which then link to the documentation stubs for the various functions, methods, and classes.  These stubs are produced automatically by [sphinx-autogen](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/ext/autosummary.html#sphinx-autogen-generate-autodoc-stub-pages).  The other Sphinx extension Py-ART uses is [numpydoc](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/numpydoc) which converts the docstring in numpy format into a format compatible with Sphinx.

There are some methods to automate the creation of a complete set of API documentation, like [sphinx-apidoc](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/invocation.html#invocation-of-sphinx-apidoc), but you need to be careful about how you design your API and I had problems getting it to work nicely with numpydoc.  In the end it seemed easier to just add the `autosummary::` blocks by hand.

You can really dig into [Sphinx](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/index.html) if you want to work out all the details.  I often find looking at how other scientific Python projects do there documentation to be quicker.  I think for Py-ART setup, I borrowed heavily from the submodules in [SciPy](https://github.com/scipy/scipy).

I actually have a kind of related question, that maybe Robert knows the answer of (and that might affect this branch too).

As far as I understand, the role of SetupError is to hold fixture details (added via the addDetail API), so consumers of fixture.setUp() can access not only the original exception but also the details that the fixture has published.

One important consumer of SetupError is arguably testtools.TestCase.useFixture(), which indeed catches MultipleExceptions and merges the fixture details into the TestCase details. Relevant code is:

````python
        try:
            fixture.setUp()
        except MultipleExceptions as e:
            if (fixtures is not None and
                    e.args[-1][0] is fixtures.fixture.SetupError):
                gather_details(e.args[-1][1].args[0], self.getDetails())
            raise
````

See [testcase.py at line 730](https://github.com/testing-cabal/testtools/blob/2.2.0/testtools/testcase.py#L730) in testtools 2.2.0.

In case of a failure in _setUp(), the above code is enough in order to have the fixture details printed out by the test runner.

However testtools **also** include the SetupError exception itself as detail (with testtoools.content.TracebackContent):

```python
    def _got_user_exception(self, exc_info, tb_label='traceback'):
        if exc_info[0] is MultipleExceptions:
            for sub_exc_info in exc_info[1].args:
                self._got_user_exception(sub_exc_info, tb_label)
            return self.exception_caught
````

See [runtest.py at line 207](https://github.com/testing-cabal/testtools/blob/2.2.0/testtools/runtest.py#L207) in testtools 2.2.0.

Now this means that even with the fix in this branch (which removes redundant SetUp context for Python 3), the typical output of a test run with a setUp failure some details would look like:

```
Tests running...
======================================================================
ERROR: test_foo.FooTest.test_foo
----------------------------------------------------------------------
foo: {{{bar}}}
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/fixtures/fixture.py", line 208, in setUp
    raise SetupError(details)
fixtures.fixture.SetupError: {'foo': <Content type=text/plain; charset="utf8", value=b'bar'>}
}}}

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/fixtures/fixture.py", line 197, in setUp
    self._setUp()
  File "/home/ubuntu/test_foo.py", line 48, in _setUp
    raise RuntimeError("boom")
RuntimeError: boom
```

Notice that the details are kind of duplicated, because they show up both as regular test case details and as "message" of the SetupError traceback being rendered.

My question is, is there any point in testtools adding SetupError as detail? Should we also change testtools to not do that, and rather only add the original error as detail?

I see the index metadata associated with the Kind rather than the Entity. And we're changing Datastore to reflect that. Index metadata lives on the Kind and should be managed via an administrative API, not when you persist a single entity.

> RE: "A parent can be another Entity (that's how a lot of people think of it anyway..." Which people? That's a sign of mis-education of users. Not something we should help propagate.

"An entity can also have an optional parent entity." See https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/datastore/modelclass

In App Engine, we pitched Entity Groups as Entities that have the same Parent entity. It was never clear from our docs that the Parent Entity didn't need to exist, and that Keys could have hierarchy regardless of whether the Entity existed or not.

> RE: "it feels a bit backwards to create a Key that isn't really a Key -- it's a partial key... " This should be addressed with the API devs, not with the library that implements it. A Key can be incomplete.

We talk about / think about private attributes / members at the level of library users, not necessarily within the library itself:

- We consider private attributes / data members as being freely available to unit tests (some of them really have no other purpose, even).  

- Because they are about the user-facing API exposed by the library, we normally avoid using them from system tests, or exposing them in example snippets.

- On a case-by-case basis, we have allowed use from within another module in the same package, in effect treating the using-module as a "friend" in the C++ / Java sense.

- At least one of our sub-packages (`gcloud.streaming`) should really be private in terms of library users, but carries a public-looking name due to a quirk/bug in `nose` (it ignores packages whose names begin with `_`).  Nevertheless, it is heavily used by `gcloud.storage.blob`.

If you need to call the API more than once a second, you could wrap it using `google.api_core.retry.Retry`, which adds retry with exponential backoff for transient errors. E.g.:

```python
from google.api_core.retry import Retry

retry_list_entries = Retry()(client.list_entries)

for entry in retry_list_entries(order_by=DESCENDING):
    print(entry)
```

Note that this will be *expensive* for any project which accumulates a large number of log entries over time:  a real-world example would likely filter the log entries, and maybe only list the N most recent on each call.

> I didn't see best-practices in the docs, so I'm not sure if I should be constructing a new client per-request and then passing-it-down through all of my db calls.

You shouldn't need to, though we are still working on making our clients threadsafe, so if your application is doing any threading you may see issues (though I don't think that is related to this issue).

@Alexis-Jacob That's correct, the error you are seeing is caused by the lack of thread-safety in `httplib2`. We recommend (for now) creating an instance of `Client` that is local to your thread / process.

It is supported... however, it's just not yet supported in the "unified" interface.
```
>>> import pathos
>>> p = pathos.helpers.mp.Pool()
>>> res = p.imap(lambda x:x**2, range(4))
>>> res.next(timeout=1)
0
>>> res.next(timeout=1)
1
>>> res.next(timeout=1)
4
>>> res.next(timeout=1)
9
>>> p.close()
>>> p.join()
```
The above library (`pathos.helpers.mp`), is a direct handle to the `multiprocess` library, which was split off from `pathos` into it's own package.

We have a test for this behaviour in `stacker/tests/test_util.py`, it will pass both in Python 2.7 and Python 3.6, however, if we replace all occurrences of `hook2` to `sss`, we should expect that the test would pass, but it will fail, even in Python 2.7. Hi Chetan,
Good to hear from you and the CloudFormation team. If I remember the last we talked was re:invent 2013 with @bobveznat  and @phobologic . We have definitely seen an uptick on Troposphere users with quite a few people contributing patches to it. The project started out as an alternative to writing json and to allow Python constructs (comments, loops, etc.) in creating the templates. It evolved into doing more object validation to get any easy issues caught at template creation time instead of template execution time. This has proven to be very useful to us and our users.

In thinking of ways the CloudFormation team and service could help us I've tried to bucket it into some major themes:

Awareness
- To be honest I'm not sure how users find out about troposphere but I have seen blog posts, slide presentations and other social media commenting about it. It would be nice to have this project listed on the AWS 3rd party library page. I had submitted a form at one point but never heard back on it.
- While the CloudFormation team presents at AWS and other venues the json templates, it might be nice to give a list of 3rd party utilities or use them as examples as well.

Keep updated
- New CloudFormation release show up out of the blue and we then scramble to figure out what changed. It also seems like docs get updated without notifications. The release notes help but still require some sleuthing to know exactly what changes. It would be nice to have a more explicit list of things changed from release to release, or updates (diffs) to the docs so we can update our code.
- If changes are coming we could coordinate and assist in integrating any code changes to Troposphere should the CloudFormation team wish to help us.

Accuracy and Thoroughness
- We have been doing our best to include as much of the AWS resources as possible and to add accurate validation. However, we don't have a way to cross check exhaustively against the docs. It would be nice to have some form of machine readable format to check resource types, property names and required against.
- Also, it might be good for the CF team to code review Troposphere for inaccuracies.

Any help the CloudFormation team wants to give us would be appreciated. This could be giving us advance warning on changes, contributing to the project and/or helping to promote it.

Thanks,
Mark, Mike and Bob

The expected value for the Heading 1 style is this:

```xml
  <w:style w:type="paragraph" w:styleId="Heading1">
    <w:name w:val="heading 1"/>
    <w:basedOn w:val="Normal"/>
    ...
  </w:style>
```

Whereas most styles follow this form (as regards name vs. style-id):

```xml
  <w:style w:type="paragraph" w:styleId="ListParagraph">
    <w:name w:val="List Paragraph"/>
    <w:basedOn w:val="Normal"/>
    ...
  </w:style>
```

The general rule is that the style name value is exactly as it appears in the Word UI.

This rule does not hold for heading styles and the 'Caption' style, which is the reason for the BableFish class.

In your case, the usual exception to the rule does not hold, and the Heading 1 name matches the UI form. I'm not sure why that is, but I believe this is the root of the problem.

I think that I've got a fix. From the note that I wrote to the mailing list:

Hi All,

Thank you for all the advice. @Andrew Wilkins: turning up logging unstuck me. :-)

Details follow, for those interested:

I've been mis-stating the root of the problem. It's not a blank value in config.yaml, it's a blank value in the "options" for haproxy in the bundle.yaml. The good news is that the config-yaml param thing is definitely the right fix for this.

The bug in python-libjuju wasn't an esoteric encoding issue -- it was simply a formatting issue, plus the fact that python-libjuju isn't interpreting all the error messages it gets back from the websocket api as errors, so I wasn't seeing the error from the gui as a traceback; I caught the error after doing a more careful read-through of the logs. I've filed a ticket against python-libjuju (https://github.com/juju/python-libjuju/issues/65) covering the issue with missing errors.

The actual formatting issue is that the websocket api wants the value of config-yaml to be a yaml string with everything under the application name. In Python, this line fixes everything: 

    options = yaml.dump({application: options})

(@Jeff Pihach: I don't see the GUI doing this, but I may be missing where it does it later on, outside of the code in your PR.)

In any case, I'll be submitting that as a patch to python-libjuju in the near future.

Meanwhile, thanks again for all your help.

~ PeteVG

Please sign your commits following these rules:
https://github.com/docker/docker/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#sign-your-work
The easiest way to do this is to amend the last commit:
~~~console
$ git clone -b "master" git@github.com:vagouzhou/docker-py.git somewhere
$ cd somewhere
$ git rebase -i HEAD~4
editor opens
change each 'pick' to 'edit'
save the file and quit
$ git commit --amend -s --no-edit
$ git rebase --continue # and repeat the amend for each commit
$ git push -f
~~~

Ammending updates the existing PR. You **DO NOT** need to open a new one.

``` 
>>>model.mean
array([1, 2])
```
rather than 
```
>>>model.mean
array([[1],
       [2]])
```
we will get different output when we do the product (consider covariance as np.eye(2) )
```
>>> # For first case 
>>> np.dot(model.mean, np.dot(model.covariance, model.mean))
5.0
>>># For the second case
>>> np.dot(model.mean.T, np.dot(model.covariance, model.mean))
array([[5.]])
```
For the earlier case I don't have to take care of the fact that output is an array, but for later I need to convert it to float (etc), to perform operations like raising it to power or something like that which we cannot do in cases of arrays. Though its my opinion.

About the presence/absence of "Regular" in the full name (id 4), @dougfelt wrote in March early this year:

> Different vendors have different conventions for whether 'Regular' appears in the full name, and for example in phase 2 this was different between Adobe and Monotype.  I don't remember how the final decision was made on this, people kept going back and forth on it and in the end I added this I think to be compatible with GlyphsApp, though I believe originally I preferred to drop 'Regular'.

Actually, for Phase 3 Noto, it's seems the opposite is true: i.e. the 'Regular' is expected to be omitted from the full name of most Noto Fonts --  except for the CJK fonts which have the `include_regular="t"` flag in the `family_name_info_p3.xml` used by the noto linter, so they are expected to have "Regular" in their full name.

ufo2ft (hence fontmake) currently never includes "Regular" in the full name, whereas Glyphs.app always does. I would like to reconcile the two behaviours somehow..

The reasons Georg prefers to keep Regular in the full name is because, its absence

> gives a hole in some font menus. It is fine for four style families but with more members I find it odd if one entry in the list has a much shorter name.

https://github.com/googlefonts/fontbakery/issues/853#issuecomment-235368066

In the latest OpenType specification for Name ID 4, they discuss about omitting "Regular" in the context of fonts like "Arial Black":

> For such non-regular fonts in which name ID 2 is specified as “Regular”, the “Regular” descriptor would generally be omitted from name ID 4.

https://www.microsoft.com/typography/otspec/name.htm#nameIDs

This seems to imply that it's only for cases like these that one shouldn't add the extra "Regular" to the full name (e.g. "Arial Black Regular"). For real Regular fonts, the general rule (full name "reflects all family and relevant subfamily descriptors") would apply.
