now I see what is going on! 

The toolbelt has a few built in tools (help, update, version and ghrn), but most of the tools are part of the project it self (it simply looks for scripts in the ./tools/build dir). 
Since this pull request is for crazyflie-lib-python you need to be located in the root of the source tree for the lib. From the list of tools in your comment, it looks like you are located in the toolbelt source directory instead (you don't really need the source for the toolbelt, unless you are interested at looking at it, since it is pulled from dockerhub). The toolbelt only acts on the project you are located in.

So, cd to your crazyflie-lib-python and run the toolbelt there. You should see this
```
Kristoffers-MacBook-Air-2:crazyflie-lib-python kristoffer$ tb
Usage:  tb tool [arguments]
The toolbelt is used to develop, test and build Bitcraze modules. When the toolbelt is called, it will first try to find the tool in the belt, after that it will try the tools in the module if the working directory is the root of a module. Module tools are executed in the context of a docker container based on the module requirements configured in the module.json config file.

Tools in the belt:
  help, -h, --help - Help
  update - Update tool belt to latest version
  version, -V, --version - Display version of the tool belt
  ghrn - Generate release notes from github milestone

Tools in the current module:
  bdist
  build
  test
  verify
```

The bitcraze/builder version is the version of the docker image that is used by the toolbelt to build your project. The toolbelt chooses a builder image based on the configuration of the project you are in (the modul.json file). In you case (when you build the toolbelt source code) it uses bitcraze/builder:12. It all looks good :-)

Just make sure you are in the correct directory and all should be good.

```sh
ssh core@$NODE0

# remove from ceph OSD
etcdctl get /deis/store/osds/10.21.1.221 #=> 0
nse deis-store-admin
... ceph osd out 0
... ceph osd crush remove osd.0
... ceph auth del osd.0
... ceph osd rm 0

# remove from deis config
etcdctl rm /deis/store/osds/10.21.1.221
etcdctl rm /deis/store/hosts/10.21.1.221
```

This still led to `HEALTH_WARN` in `ceph -s`, then I did `ceph mon remove`:

```sh
$ ceph -s
     health HEALTH_WARN
$ ceph mon stat
  e4: 4 mons at ...
$ ceph mon remove ip-10-21-1-221.ap-southeast-2.compute.internal

  e5: 3 mons at ...
$ ceph -s
     health HEALTH_OK
```

Hi Sergey! A factor of 20 is a nice result! Thanks for providing the ODBC trace, and alos thanks for interpreting the results! Turbodbc uses information about the columns in the result set to determine the number of rows to retrieve in bulk if you use a `read_buffer_size` specified in Megabytes.

According to the [ODBC reference](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/odbc/reference/syntax/sqlsetstmtattr-function), drivers are supposed to overwrite this value if the value exceeds their maximum:

> If the specified rowset size exceeds the maximum rowset size supported by the data source, the driver substitutes that value and returns SQLSTATE 01S02 (Option value changed).

It seems that SQLAnywhere does not do that :-). It makes sense that reducing the buffer size will reduce the risk of the error you reported popping up. The "best" number will depend on the smallest table, however, where small is with respect to the number of bytes per row.

I'm wondering if we could accomplish a similar journal mechanism using a combination of the [`BatchDB`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/160) concept, and just rolling back the `state_root` on reversions instead of tracking all the actual database changes.

- set a checkpoint in the batch db, take note of the `state_root`

@alukach -- Great sleuthing! That looks like it explains our problem as well, because our database setup process is similar:

- Create `template_postgis`
- Create django app database from `template_postgis`
- Run `CREATE EXTENSION hstore;` on django app database
- Run`CREATE EXTENSION hstore;` on `template_postgis`

In other words, `template_postgis` is not hstore-enabled until _after_ the django app database is created, which then (apparently) causes the postgis backend to erroneously believe that the test database is not hstore-enabled when in fact it is, while the postgresql_psycopg2 backend correctly detects and utilizes the hstore extension.

Sure, no problem. @bkreider, @kalefranz, your input is welcome too. Honestly I think this code is in need of a more complete refactor but this fix should do for now. Here is the basic problem, and yes, we can talk about it on Tuesday.

When we are _normalizing_ channels---that is, going from a "short" channel names to full channel URLs---we need to add the binstar token whenever `add_anaconda_token` is True (by default). This allows users to enter _just_ the short channel name into their `.condarc`, even if it's a private channel that requires a login to access. In the opposite direction, whenever we are _canonicalizing_ channels---that is, going from the full URL to a "short" channel name---we need to _strip off_ the binstar tokens. 

Both of these token operations should take applied _only_ to `anaconda.org`; or, if the user is connecting to the enterprise product, to the domain name they've given to their local repository.

Unfortunately, it looks like there is a baked-in assumption in `normalize_channels` that `channel_alias`, if specified, refers to the token-enabled repository. But in theory, it doesn't need to be. A user could construct a local, token-free repository, point `channel_alias` to that, and *still* use Anaconda. But if they do that, then the tokenization process clobbers `channel_alias`.

The fix I've proposed here takes care of that. But what we really need to do here is strip out the explicit references to `binstar.org` and `anaconda.org` from this file altogether. The repository domain name is going to be returned by `get_binstar().domain` anyway, so there's no need for that anymore.

OK, I understand what's going on here. In order to select the best set of packages to install, conda employs a "version metric" that assigns a value to every version of a package. The smaller the value, the more recent the package. So for instance, `foo-1.0.0` gets a metric of `1`, and `foo-2.0.0` gets a metric of `0`. 

However, it currently applies this metric to  _all_ packages, _including dependencies_. If it installs `foo-1.0.0`, the total metric is just `1`, because there are no dependencies. If I install `foo-2.0.0`, the metric is much larger, because it is pulling in other dependencies.

I agree this is suboptimal behavior, but it's not clear the best way to handle it. I think that if both version of the package had the same set of dependencies this wouldn't be an issue.

The problem, I'm afraid, is that conditional depdendencies are part of 4.4, not 4.3. I was mistaken about the development timeline for that.

Unfortunately, that means that if the ecosystem needs to support the `conflicts`-style capability in 4.3 we're going to have to find an alternative approach.

What I would suggest in this case is that you create two packages with the same _package name_ but different _build strings_; say, for instance,
```
prgenv-0.0.0-gnu_linux64_edison.tar.bz2
prgenv-0.0.0-gnu_linux64_cori.tar.gz2
```
Giving them the same package name ensures that only one of them can be installed in an environment at the same time. Then the packages that depend on one or the other of these packages can just pin to the proper build string; e.g.
```
depends:
  -   prgenv * gnu_linux64_edison
```

The way to implement this is to create a new `PathAction` that creates this stub executable if it doesn't exist.  We need to watch out for a gotcha with the `reverse()` method, because something that triggers `reverse()` on `conda create` should remove the file, while something that triggers `reverse()` on `conda install` probably shouldn't.

Basically, we need a flag in the `execute()` method to indicate whether the file was created, or whether it already existed.  If it was newly created, `reverse()` should just remove it.

Thanks for raising this issue. You are absolutely right that this is inconsistent.

> `elasticsearch-py` raises immediatelly but `elasticsearch-py-async` will report about problem in sniffing in the past.

This *feels* ok to me since with async we gain the option of sniffing in the background (which I believe is better than what `elasticsearch-py` does now). Then we need to raise the error somewhere (unless we suppress it and just log it, also an option).

I think the proper solution is to change the sniffing logic wait for first *successful* request to return, ignoring the rest (just relying on the standard logging mechanism for discovery). Right now the `_get_sniff_data` method is waiting for first task to finish (via `asyncio.wait(..., return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED)`) which will most likely be the failed request. That way the error will be very unlikely - essentially only when all the requests fail.

Another option would be to document the `invoke_sniffing` method and encourage people to run it on their own (using a timer for example) if they really care about how this is done (waiting for the future right away, letting it run on background, ...).

Currently ACR doesn't have the delete API enabled yet.

The current implementation of DELETE API may not be what customer expects. The blob delete only deletes the link to the real image blob (since the real blob can still be referenced by other images). Thus it won’t reduce the storage usage much, and it may leave some garbage around.

1)	Delete manifest (image). It will delete the specific version of the manifest of the image.
2)	Delete blob. It will delete the reference to the blob from the specified image, but not the blob data itself.

ACR was considering to turn it on, but didn't since not sure if it’s the desired behavior…Besides, we have been thinking of the alternative where we do garbage collection of unreferenced blob when delete. This is still under design…

Would like to hear more feedback before we act on it…

If you check [the docs](http://azure.github.io/azure-storage-python/ref/azure.storage.queue.queueservice.html) in the queue service about endpoint suffix, you'll see examples: "Defaults Azure (core.windows.net). Override this to use the China cloud (core.chinacloudapi.cn)." Similarly, if you check the existing default value for endpoint_suffix, it equals 'core.windows.net' as promised in the docs. 

So, the endpoint suffix you're using is not formatted correctly which results in the URL not resolving. If you switch to 'core.windows.net', the existing default, everything works fine. However since it's the default, there's no real point to specifying this. If you'd like to use the China endpoint, use 'core.chinacloudapi.cn'.

Proper task local implementation is impossible without Python 3.7 or custom task factory (the state should be cloned on forking a new task.
I would like to keep aiohttp explicit in this matter, at least until Python 3.7 support.
If you need to track a third party library request -- the library should support it by accepting `TraceContext` parameter. It is a part of configuration along with requested URLs, SSL context and other things.
Otherwise making a mess becomes very trivial. Let's imagine we use several libraries: AWS client, slack notifier, a client to make REST queries to other microservices. With global context I cannot track, say, microservices only or process their requests differently. 
The other problem is that nothing is for free. Tracing slows down request handling a little, enabling a tracing for particular clients only is important thing.

Tracked it down to [this bit](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/v1.5.2/lib/matplotlib/backends/web_backend/nbagg_mpl.js#L165), where nbagg is turning a shift-enter key event into a `j` key event to send to Jupyter, which assumes a few things that aren't necessarily true:

1. that while the figure is focused, its cell is selected
2. that `j` is still bound to select-next

The 4.0-4.1 change here is that the containing cell is force-selected when the figure is focused in 4.0, whereas 4.1 does not enforce this, and leaves the selection on the next cell. At that point, when `j` is struck, it selects cell n + 2, because the cursor wasn't where nbagg assumed it would be.

matplotlib/matplotlib#6752 fixes this to directly select the cell after the one containing the figure, regardless of current selection.

Hmm. This raises an important question: is the primitive type always static, or is it allowed to vary according to, say, a parameter on the field or the value being exported?

I was able to find one case where the type depends on the value: the new `TimestampType` returns an `int` if the value has a zero microsecond component; otherwise a `float` is returned. I think this can be classified as a bug — a `float` should always be returned. But is this kind of behavior always a bug?

Unless someone can provide a legitimate use case, I'd be inclined to rule that the primitive type cannot depend on context-specific circumstances such as the value itself or options specified at export time, as that would be incompatible with static schema descriptions.

As another example, consider a variant of `IPv4Type` that exports the value as an integer instead of a dot-decimal string (let's pretend it doesn't inherit from `StringType`). Would it be mandatory to implement this as a separate type instead of a parameter on `IPv4Type`? If not, then `PRIMITIVE_TYPE` may sometimes have to be a property and should not look like a constant.

Assigning exactly one primitive datatype to every Schematics type certainly feels like a good idea. For example, we have `UTCDateTimeType` instead of an option on `DateTimeType`. But I'm not yet sure whether mandating such arrangement is *always* appropriate.

And finally, it may be a reasonable expectation that the native type be inspectable as well, in which case the same considerations apply.

That would be great!

On an email list, some DeepMind people pointed out a way to implement GGN-vector products with reverse mode and `stop_gradients`/`getval`, essentially exploiting the fact that the GGN matrix looks like the Hessian except with a term set to zero (which we can achieve by calling `getval` to make something look like a constant). Here's my [current sketch of an implementation](https://gist.github.com/mattjj/842383cc908747b6a90889deee9f2492), though I bet it can be improved.

Regarding purely reverse-mode vs mixed-mode strategies, I suspect that the story for GGN-vector products might end up being similar to the story for HVPs: namely, there should at least be significant memory savings (~2x), though it's less clear if there are any FLOP savings to be expected. I'm still a bit confused on those issues. However, the code in that gist is currently doing two forward passes on f, which I think means it's more expensive in FLOPs too.

If we wanted to do GGN products in the inner loop of an optimization algorithm, like in K-FAC or HF, I suspect that the savings from a mixed-mode implementation would be very much worthwhile. Having that available is a distinct advantage for autograd right now.

I'm sorry for the delay in getting back.  I just finished looking at this today and it looks like the rules for the Windows command shell is different than many *nix/OS X shells in how it handles quoting.  Rather than single quotes starting a quoted string, they are received by the Windows shell as literal single quotes.  So the export command you are performing is outputting the header as `cif'       'nr_rachunku_bik' ...` (and the header is what load uses to infer the delimiter and column names).  I think we should probably just remove all quote characters from the command-line delimiter field, but in the interim you can make this work by just removing the quotes in the command to make it look like this:

```giraffez export DDHDP01P.AG_KHD_4463_2_BIK_NOWA output_file.txt -d \t```

If you don't have an app factory, you can just return the instance of your app from your module. For example if you configure your app in a file called `hello.py` [as shown in the Flask Getting Started docs][1]:

```python
from flask import Flask
app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route("/")
def hello():
    return "Hello World!"
```

Then you can just return the `app` attribute of the module:

```python
from pytest import fixture
import hello

@fixture
def app():
    return hello.app
```

[1]: http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/quickstart/#a-minimal-application

sklearn-crfsuite works with a very different input type than built-in sklearn classifiers; it takes a list of sequences of feature dicts. First, it does sequence prediction problem, so it accepts a list of sequences of varying length, not just a list/matrix of instances. Second, it doesn its own feature vectorization, so it doesn't take matrices as an input. 

You can't use it instead of e.g. sklearn.svm.SVC (and vice versa), as they're solving different tasks. Check the tutorial - https://sklearn-crfsuite.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html.

To save a model you can use joblib or pickle:

```py
import joblib

# save:
joblib.dump(crf, 'mycrf.joblib')  #, compress=4) 

# load:
crf = joblib.load('mycrf.joblib')
```

Here is how it looks like with pickle:

```py
import pickle

# save:
with open('mycrf.joblib', 'wb') as f:
    pickle.dump(crf, f)

# load:
with open('mycrf.joblib', 'rb') as f:
    crf = pickle.load(f)
```

From the latest Beta Program email:

# How to use the client

The Let's Encrypt client supports a number of different "plugins" that can be used to obtain and/or install certificates.  A few examples of the options are included below:

If you're running Apache on a recent Debian-based OS, you can try the Apache plugin, which automates both obtaining and installing certs:

  ./letsencrypt-auto --apache --server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory --agree-dev-preview 

To obtain a cert using a "standalone" webserver (you may need to temporarily stop your exising webserver) for example.com and www.example.com:

  ./letsencrypt-auto certonly -a standalone -d example.com -d www.example.com --server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory --agree-dev-preview 

To obtain a cert using the "webroot" plugin, which can work with the webroot of any webserver software:

  ./letsencrypt-auto certonly -a webroot --webroot-path /var/www/example -d example.com -d www.example.com -server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory --agree-dev-preview 

NB: curretly the webroot plugin can only obtain certs for several domains simultaneously if they share a webroot.

The CA/Browser Forum rule in response to this, as it stands, would only allow issuance of EV certificates to onion names (which is what Digicert does).

We will perhaps have to join the CA/B Forum and make a motion there to extend the permissibility of issuance for DV certificates (which is what we issue). Alternately, I've seen a discussion about having the Tor Browser recognize some kind of special-case certificates that are only for its consumption, not for other browsers, and having Let's Encrypt or some other entity issue Tor Browser-friendly DV certificates (that other browsers won't consume) for onion names.

Salesforce use only thee builtin one-to-one relationships: two times relationship to FeedItem and one relationship from ApexEmailNotification to User. Nobody can create a custom one-to-one relationships.

I remember from Salesforce forums that it is mostly recommended to set one direction by a lookup field or master detail relationship field, both variants with "unique Id" checkbox, plus to set a reverse lookup field, (e. g. by a trigger for insert/update/delete and set this field read-only for users).

```
class Avl(Model):
    veiculo = models.ForeignKey('Veiculo', models.DO_NOTHING, db_column='Ve_culo__c', blank=True,
```

> Loading and then dumping that data should result in the exact same value for that field. But it doesn't.

It is the same value with different encodings. `Z` and `+00:00` are equivalent.

```py
import dateutil

dt = dateutil.parser.parse('2018-07-25T00:00:00Z')
dt.tzinfo()
# tzutc()
dt.isoformat()
# 2018-07-25T00:00:00+00:00
```

`dateutil`'s `parser` is passing the timezone correctly and `datetime.isoformat()` is allowed to represent `Z` as `+00:00`. If you are interfacing with a system that doesn't accept `+00:00` as a timezone, it probably isn't compliant. Otherwise it is a personal preference that can be achieved with a custom format.

The steps for building the `hapt_data.sframe` are indeed missing from the current documentation, and you can find them here: https://github.com/apple/turicreate/issues/41#issuecomment-350538212

As for file paths (csv files to be loaded, saved sframe files and saved model files) - please note that all of the methods below except any full or relative path you'll provide them:
```
sf = tc.SFrame.read_csv('some/path/some_file.csv')
sf.save('some/other/path/my_data.sf')
loaded_sf = tc.SFrame('some/other/path/my_data.sf')
model.save('new_desired_path/my_model.model`)
```

The current path your running your python from doesn't matter - as long as you provide a valid path where you want sframes\models to be saved to or loaded from.

Also, it is not required to run your python script from within a specific path (`venv `or any other). 
 The only advice is to activate the virtualenv in which you've installed turicreate by doing: `source ~/venv/bin/activate`.
After you do - feel free to write your own `.py` script or ipython notebook. Such script will usually begin with:
```
import turicreate as tc
data = tc.Sframe.read_csv('some_data.csv')
#...
model = tc.activity_classifier.create(data, ...)
#...
```

Feel free to ask for any further clarifications.

The CEC library doesn’t contain the parameters that are needed for the SAPM.  It only has parameters for the CEC performance model.

NREL _might_ (I’m not sure) have added functionality to translate from CEC model parameters to SAPM parameters.  I know PVsyst has done this, but the translation is suspect in my opinion.  It works by computing modeled IV curves (using the PVsyst model), picking off Isc, Vmp/Imp and Voc, then fitting the SAPM equations to these _modeled_ points.  So you get a SAPM model which emulates the PVsyst model including any biases, rather than measured performance.

@scottfisk @jerel 
Thanks for tracking this down and getting back to me so quickly.

I'd still label this as a bug currently. If drf serializers are not to be used with jsonapi viewsets, then an exception should be raised much earlier on, rather than this subtle mistake in the output.

However, I really hope there's a better solution than just not supporting drf serializers. Part of the beauty of this package is that it can be used as a partial drop-in replacement to drf and everything just works.

Here's my company's use case:
We have a v1 API using drf. Recently we've been transitioning to v2 using jsonapi. This has been an incremental change. So taking my example from earlier, imagine the `Hand` model is in one app and `Finger` is in another. We make a v2 api endpoint for `Hand`'s app, building fresh serializers and viewsets for it (using `restframework_json_api.serializers`, say), however `Finger`'s app is not yet updated. So we've been able to just have `HandSerializer`s `included_serializers` use `FingerSerializer` from the v1 api and everything would still work.

I'm not sure exactly how jsonapi's `ModelSerializer` differs so much from drf's that would prevent this 'backwards compatibility', but I think it would be worth trying to support both.

From doing some of my own digging I see that `relation_type = utils.get_related_resource_type(field)` ([line 105](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/commit/0aedffb88fc7009f95e8ca35ec65a4e594d6c685#diff-e2e58e3e5c345fc3b14c160b3a00a64cR105)) always finds the wrong type for the field, regardless of whether drf or jsonapi serializers are used. It's just that in the jsonapi case [line 162](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/commit/0aedffb88fc7009f95e8ca35ec65a4e594d6c685#diff-e2e58e3e5c345fc3b14c160b3a00a64cR162) overrides it. So I have a suspicion there's a bug with `utils.get_related_resource_type`, which only came to light as a result of the recent renderer changes. I'll investigate this further and get back to you.

Anyway, I see two courses of action here:
- Raise exception when jsonapi viewset attempts to use serializer not defined by jsonapi. I feel the problem is too subtle to just mention lack of drf serializer support in the docs.
- Extend tests to consider both jsonapi and drf serializers (in their simplest form), and fix whatever is going wrong with the current implementation (be it with `utils.get_related_resource_type` or elsewhere).

If I submit a PR for the second option will you accept it? :)

```python
from sacred import Experiment

     ada_lr['epochs'] = [100]
```

The core of the issue is that I couldn't get things to work correctly for "multi-configuration generators" (i.e. Visual Studio) due to some limiations in CMake so in order to make the behaviour consistent between all CMake builds I just put the ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` in ``src/api/dotnet`` in the build directory.

In hindsight that might not have been the best decision. Perhaps instead

* ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` should go in the root build directory like ``libz3.so`` does for single configuration generators. For multi-configuration generators a problem will occur where it isn't possible to have multiple versions of ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` (this means if you change the build type in Visual Studio the old version of ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` will be overwritten) but we have that problem already in the code in the ``master`` branch. Note this isn't a problem for ``libz3.so`` which is put in its own directory (e.g. ``Debug``) depending on Visual Studio's build type but I currently can't replicate that behaviour for building ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` in CMake.

* If on Windows and in a single configuration build pass the ``/linkresource:`` flag to mimic what the existing Python build system does. I didn't do this before because ``csc.exe`` is weird. It seems to require the the argument to ``/linkresource`` be a file name and not a path. If you give ``/linkresource:`` an absolute path it does not complain but when an attempt is made to link against a ``.NET`` application against ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` the copy of ``libz3.dll`` fails...

If these seem like good ideas I can create a PR for them if you'd like.

I guess I got confused as I expected Z3's API functions get_numeral_exponent_string and get_numeral_exponent_int to return an integer whose binary representation matches that of the IEEE 754 exponent bits. However, if I understand correctly, the API functions instead return the integer which may serve as the exponent in deriving the actual real value.

The two are nearly the same - apart from the transition from normal to denormal numbers. For very small normals, both have a "mathematical" exponent of -126 (for 32 bit floats), as you stated. However, in the binary IEEE 754 format, these two cases differ: Denormals have only zero bits in the exponent field, whereas small normals have one as an exponent. This is where I got trapped - I expected to receive -127 (with bias) or 0 (without bias) for denormals. But not -126.

That brings me back to my previous remark: For us, it would be very nice to have API functions that allow to extract the IEEE 754 bit-wise representation of a floating point numeral. That would allow us to use results obtained from Z3 in our own IEEE 754 softfloat library directly, i.e. without having to first convert into an arbitrary precision Q and then back to IEEE 754... 

Something like get_exponent_bits and get_significand_bits both taking a floating point numeral and returning a string containing an unsigned decimal whose binary representation matches that of the IEEE 754 encoding would be great:

Example:
get_exponent_bits (fp #b0 #b00000000 #b11111111111111111111111) = "0"
get_exponent_bits (fp #b0 #b00000001 #b11111111111111111111111) = "1"
get_exponent_bits (fp #b0 #b00000011 #b11111111111111111111111) = "3"
get_significand_bits (fp #b0 #b00000001 #b11111111111111111111111) = "8388607"

Yes you can re-use the bodies, but you need to be careful about the variables and their indexes. Assuming that you see at some point in time the original, correct quantified expression, you can extract names/sorts/etc by using the functions called `Z3_get_quantifier_*`, e.g., `Z3_get_quantifier_num_bound`, which will tell you how many bound variables there are. Your program needs to be able to access libz3.dll as well as libz3java.dll (or .so, .dylib). Both of them need to be in your PATH and/or Java wants to see libz3java.dll in it's `java.library.path`, i.e., on the command line like so: `java -Djava.library.path=C:\path\to\dll`.

Note that all DLL/so/dylib's have to have the same bit-ness as your Java installation. I.e., a 32-bit Java will not load a 64-bit DLL and it may not report any useful error messages if that is the case (it will just throw an UnsatisfiedLinkError without any other information pointing to the underlying issue).

There are two ways:

1. You can use print f.sexpr(). This prints the formula in the SMT-LIB2 syntax. It uses let-expressions to compress common sub-expressions.
2. You can use set_pp_option(max_visited=10000000) set_pp_option(max_depth=1000000) to avoid elipses.

Generally speaking, the python pretty printer takes more overhead than the native SMT-LIB2 pretty printer, and is really best targeted at small formulas.

You can also create a solver object, assert the formula, and then use the sexpr() method on the solver object to pretty print the asserted formulas as a benchmark.

Awesome, thanks for your comments @jberdine! I agree, we could take this down into the stubs where ocaml can't look into the code, so it won't collect anything. I kind of like the idea of using caml_register_generational_global_root, but it's not particularly nice either. Do you know whether that's compile-time and thus cheap or runtime expensive?

Formally speaking, it's actually OK if OCaml collects the "high-level" list-of-ast object. By creating a second copy of the native object, we should increment the ref counts, because we do in fact hold another reference, i.e., *_lton should actually call inf_ref on each object, and we delete the list-of-native object after the function call (i.e., call dec_ref on each). We could simply introduce a new data structure which calls the dec_ref for each element when the list/array goes out of scope (e.g., put a finalizer onto the array?), but OCaml users seem not to be partial to object-orientation it seems. As Martin said, this will also create loads of API calls which isn't particularly attractive either.

Originally the idea was to create a new luxury, highest-level structure as in the other APIs, for OCaml without the need for camlidl. But, almost nothing of that idea remains, so perhaps it's best if we just revert the native layer to what camlidl used to generate, and perhaps even use it, if it comes with all the different ocaml flavours now. We could still keep the module structure in z3.ml/mli which people seem to like.

This is even better than what I expected : after studying the source of [`zipfile.py`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/zipfile.py), this is what I understood:

when opening a file in a zipfile, it will create a `_SharedFile` object that locks on reading, and does not support seeking, but maps the virtual position in the file to the actual position in the Zip file stream. So we could bypass the `_SharedFile` and implement locking ourselves in a custom `ZipExtFile`, which would be similar to what I did when I implemented `ISOFS`. That way, we could have real seeking as long as the underlying Zip file stream supports streaming.
Alas, there doesn't seem to be any way of doing this.

```python
import os

from pynwb import NWBFile
from pynwb.misc import UnitTimes

class ContainerRoundTrip():
    
    def __init__(self, container, filename='temp.nwb'):
        self.container = container
        self.filename = filename
        
    def __enter__(self):
        nwbfile = NWBFile(source='source',
                  session_description='session_description',
                  identifier='identifier',
                  session_start_time=datetime.now(),
                  file_create_date=datetime.now(),
                  institution='institution',
                  lab='lab')
        module = nwbfile.create_processing_module('test', source='source', description='description')
        module.add_container(self.container)
        
        with NWBHDF5IO(self.filename, 'w') as io:
            io.write(nwbfile)
        self.io = NWBHDF5IO(self.filename, 'r')
        nwbfile = self.io.read()
        container = nwbfile.modules['test'].containers[0]
        return container

    def __exit__(self, type, value, traceback):
        self.io.close()
        os.remove(self.filename)
```

This made me curious about escape codes and how they're handled when mixed between stderr and stdout. Of course it's literally what you said. Example script:

```python
from colorama import Fore, Back, Style
import sys

sys.stderr.write(Fore.BLUE + 'blue!\n')
sys.stderr.flush()
print(Style.DIM + 'STDOUT DIM')
print(Style.RESET_ALL)
sys.stderr.flush()
sys.stdout.flush()
print('back to normal now')
sys.stderr.write('woo\n')
```

> The question is that the bin file like nginx will not treat the URL I request as an input.

By creating a SimProcedure for the URL processing function and hooking at correct places, you can make the parsed URL treated as an input in angr.

But I don't think angr is able to perform a full concolic execution on software like nginx or Apache httpd. There are multiple barriers, the biggest ones are:
- angr does not support multithreading or multi-processing, on which I think nginx or httpd all rely.
- angr lacks support of many syscalls, some of which are essential to run nginx or httpd.
- angr does not scale well to programs of that size. To be honest, I believe this is a scientific problem that many people are working on, and angr is simply not the correct tool to use as of now. In the future angr _will_ be able to concolically execute nginx or httpd, but not tomorrow or next month.

Even if you can eventually make angr work on nginx or Apache httpd, I believe you will spend a lot of time and effort on that, which, I'm not sure if it is a good idea. But it's up to you.

My suggestion is to use angr to concolically execute small parts of nginx or Apache httpd, like tens or hundreds of functions, with clearly defined symbolic/concrete input and such. A tutorial on this topic, as I see it, will be *very* helpful for a lot of people.

   * create a specific database like `mydatabase` with UTF8 encoding
   * set your database to `UTC` timezone (`ALTER DATABASE mydatabase SET timezone TO 'UTC';`)
   * change your backend settings to `kinto.core.storage.postgresql` (see settings docs)
   * change your backend url to something like `postgresql://scott:tiger@localhost:5432/mydatabase`
  * run `kinto migrate`
  * restart kinto

R uses a 'MODWT' transform, whereas MATLAB uses the "algorithme à trous" to perform shift-invariant transforms. The difference is in how the 'stretched' wavelet filters are calculated.

MATLAB uses something like this, with `j` being the level, and `g` the approximation filter. The detail filter `h` is calculated in the same way.
```
g[j] = numpy.zeros(len(g[0]) * 2 ** (j - 1))
g[j][::2 ** (j - 1)] = g[0]
```

MODWT uses different filters (let's say `g'` and `h'` for approximation and detail respectively). There is a public introduction available [here](http://faculty.washington.edu/dbp/PDFFILES/4-Lund-A4.pdf), and derivation in *Percival & Walden, 2000, Wavelet Methods for Time Series Analysis, Equation 95e*. Note these refer to `g` and `h` as defined above.
```
g'[j] = convolve(g[0], g[1], ... g[j])
h'[j] = convolve(g'[j], h[j])
```

Note that the MODWT filters should allow one to directly compute the `j`th level DWT (with appropriate downsampling). It doesn't actually reduce the number of operations (the filter is `2**j` times longer) but removes the need to allocate scratch space for each level of the transform and *probably* is cache-friendlier since there is only one pass over the input.

Yeah, this is all very confusing unfortunately.. Currently, both WARC and HTTP headers are encoded as ISO-8859-1/Latin-1
https://github.com/webrecorder/warcio/blob/master/warcio/statusandheaders.py#L148

I think the HTTP headers should be whatever the original encoding was, which is probably ISO-8859-1. [https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3333/#unicode-issues](PEP 3333) also assumes ISO-8859-1 for HTTP headers, so I think that is correct.

For WARC headers, it seems that it should be using UTF-8 as the default, based on this (slightly ambiguos wording):

`Although UTF-8 characters are allowed, the 'encoded-word' mechanism of [RFC2047] may also be used when writing WARC fields and shall also be understood by WARC reading software.
`

Heritrix seems to use UTF-8, while warcprox uses ISO-8859-1/Latin-1.

In conclusions, I think what we should have is:
- warcinfo contents as UTF-8
- WARC headers as UTF-8
- http headers as ISO-8859-1/Latin-1

I am currently investigating solutions to this issue.  Here is the problem we're facing:

1. If we want to enable pretty-printing, the magics necessarily need to have a side effect.  So Livy would give us a dataframe, and the spark magic would return `None`. Returning `None` silences the kernel so nothing else would be printed to the screen.

2. We can't return the result of the magic because if we did, then that would frequently cause results for an invocation to be printed twice: once (pretty-printed) by the magics, and once (improperly formatted) by the kernel.

3. However, this prevents users from being able to do something like `x = %sql SELECT * FROM...`, or from doing `%sql SELECT * FROM ...` and using the `Out[]` array to get the dataframe from an invocation.  This limits the usefulness of the magics.

If we all agree this functionality is necessary, then we have a few options.

1. Give different commands different names.  So we would have one command for running `sql` queries and pretty-printing the results, and one other command for running `sql` queries and getting the results of the query as a dataframe.  Alejandro and I think this is a pretty messy solution.

2.  Add an option to silence and return the output of the magic.  So for example one could write `%sql SELECT * FROM ... -r`, which would have no output and simply return the dataframe.  What's unfortunate here is that you then can't pretty-print the results and also save the results as a dataframe, since silencing and returning the output are captured in the same option.

3.  Similarly to option 2, add two options: `-r`, which returns the dataframe instead of `None`, and `-q`, which silences the pretty-printer.  This has all the power that one would need from the system, but now pretty-printing the output while also capturing the results as a dataframe (which might be a common pattern for some users) becomes pretty verbose: `%sql SELECT * FROM ... -r -q`.  

What do you think?  Do we want the more verbose but more powerful options for the magics?

First, have a look at our [sample data](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/sample-data.md) which are a collection of recorded sequences that can be used to use the library without a device ([See here how](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/src/media/readme.md#rs2playback)).

Second, at the moment there is only the `get_depth_sensor` method since it is a bit unique (returning a `rs2::depth_sensor` rather than `rs2::sensor`). as the `get_a_sensor_from_a_device` describes, you can iterate a device's sensors using: ` std::vector<rs2::sensor> sensors = dev.query_sensors();`, then you can query the sensor for its capabilities to understand which sensor it is exactly:

1. Using the `.supports(rs2_option)` to know what control you have over the sensor
2. Using the `.supports(rs2_camera_info)` - static information of the sensor
3. Using the `.get_stream_profiles()` - a list of supported streams the sensor can provide

A "color sensor" is not well defined at the moment so to get the RGB camera of D400 or SR300, you can either query it by name ("RGB Camera") or query if it supports some option (e.g. white_balance perhaps?), or a specific stream type (e.g. `sensor.get_stream_profiles()[0].stream_type() == RS2_STREAM_COLOR`)

Hey @phillbaker, 

no `run_before` does not solve anything. As the data model is crafted today, you cannot deploy one table before the other. What needs to happen is (for example, can be the other way around)
* deploy the `AccessToken` swapped model WITHOUT the `source_refresh_token` column
* deploy the `RefreshToken` swapped model
* add the `source_refresh_token` column to the swapped `AccessToken` model table.

You basically need to manually amend the auto-generated migration

This is an extract of what I ended up with

```python
operations = [
    migrations.CreateModel(
        name='AccessToken',
        fields=[
            ('id', models.BigAutoField(primary_key=True, serialize=False)),
            ('expires', models.DateTimeField()),
            ('scope', models.TextField(blank=True)),
            ('created', models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)),
            ('updated', models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)),
```

The changes in #1653 are more strict than what Thread 1.1 Section 10.6.2 specifies.  The changes in #1653 requires the IPv6 source address to be an RLOC or ALOC.  However, Thread 1.1 Section 10.6.2 does not require the IPv6 source address to be RLOC/ALOC if the destination address is an RLOC/ALOC or link-local address.

> A TMF CoAP message sent to the Thread Management UDP port (:MM) MUST comply with at least one of these addressing rules:
> * The IPv6 source address is an RLOC or ALOC.
> * The IPv6 destination address is an RLOC or ALOC. The IPv6 destination address is a Link-Local address.
> * Some of the individual TMF Message definitions will also impose specific, stricter rules for addressing.

Note the "at least one of these" wording above.

For background, the rules in Section 10.6.2 were defined with the goal of allowing an application protocol to utilize the same :MM port.  This implies that any "application" wishing to send/receive TMF messages on the :MM port must be aware of RLOC/ALOC addresses.  See [SPEC-498](https://threadgroup.atlassian.net/browse/SPEC-498) for more discussion around this.

We can argue whether or not Section 10.6.2 should be made more strict, but this needs to happen within the Thread Group.

Oh, I agree that in C++ it's very useful (pybind itself is full of `void *`s).  The issue is that we really have no way for them to survive the round trip from C++ -> Python -> C++ again; even if you return one, the pointer itself is discarded and you just end up with `None` in Python.

Local variables that live in the current stack frame will be passed by reference when supplied to handle::call(). Since these expire immediately after the parent function returns, pybind11 would really set things up for a catastrophe if the default policy was to always reference things (even for lvalue references).

On the other hand, in many C++ codebases, pointers in function arguments are used to refer to things that are located on the heap, so the assumption is that the user is at least in some way aware of lifetime issues and will override the reference policy to something else if that is not intended.

There is no default policy that will make everyone happy (hence the ability to override things everywhere). This one is just my very subjective choice for what happens to work 99% in my own codebases ;)

For the restartable tasks I'm using this nomenclature:

```
A -- B -- C -- B -- D

A is the mediated transfer initiator.
D is the mediated transfer target.
B and C are mediators.

Each mediator has someone that will pay the transfer and someone that the mediator is paying to, I'm calling the hop paying `payer` and the receiving `payee`. Each mediator will only forward a mediated transfer if it's given a valid payer transfer, these (payer, payee) are grouped in transfer pairs.

For B:
  The (A, C) transfer pair, A as the payer and C as the payee.
  The (C, D) transfer pair, C as the payer and D as the payee.
For C:
  The (B, B) transfer pair, B is both the payer and the payee.
```

It is social-core's default behavior and one that can be [configured for different needs](http://python-social-auth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration/settings.html#username-generation). Also worth noting that the username is only generated once: if I login with my Steam username as *Matoking*, Django will create an User with the username *Matoking*. If I then log off, change my Steam nickname to *Atomking*, my Django user will still have the username *Matoking*.

If you want to use the SteamID as the username, you could override the [pipeline](http://python-social-auth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pipeline.html#authentication-pipeline) step responsible for generating the username: the step you need to replace is *social_core.pipeline.user.get_username*. The SteamID should be exposed through the *details* keyword argument.

@istoica For this pattern:
```python
# Actor 1 -----S1.1----------------S1.2---------|--- -> timeline
#           ^                   ^               |
#           | T1.1              | T1.3 (finish) |
# Batch 1 --------------------------------------|--- -> timeline
#               | T1.2                          |
#               v                               |
# Actor 2 ---------S2.1----S2.2-----------------|--- -> timeline
#                       ^                       |
#                       | T2.1                  |
# Batch 2 --------------------------------------|--- -> timeline
#                                               |
#                                    (cleanup moment of batch 1)
```
`S2.1` can NOT be checkpointed when batch 1 finishes, because at this moment Actor 2 is on the state `S2.2`. Although we can reconstruct the Actor state `S2.1` by re-executing tasks, I think this is too consuming -- almost re-run all tasks under highly interleaved batches.

On the other words, when `T1.2` finishes, we do not know it is the last task of batch 1 in Actor 2, thus we do not have enough information to decide whether to checkpoint this Actor at this moment.

If I misunderstand the scenario, please point it out. Thanks a lot.

Thanks for bringing this up, this is an important problem.

Copying the below from https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/907#issuecomment-327563088.

Our serialization code is written in C++ and is normally quite fast when mapping Python objects to the Arrow format. However, the C++ code only handles primitive types and lists/dicts/tuples. So when it encounters something it doesn't know how to serialize (e.g., a set), then it falls back to using cloudpickle (so it leaves C++ and goes back to Python).

There are a couple things that could be done.
- Add a special case for sets in the C++ code (this probably makes sense, but it won't work if you replace the sets in your example with some custom Python class).
- We need to generally optimize this code path more.

I think *yield point* may actually be a good phrase -- not because of the internal `yield` keyword, but because the task yields control to the trio scheduler. Try this on for size -- it's a rewriting of the ['a yield point is two things'](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-core.html#yield-points) bit. 

> At a yield point, the task briefly yields control back to the trio scheduler (usually `trio.run()`). Two things happen at a yield point:
> 
> 1. The task yields control to the trio scheduler, which then has the option of switching to a different task. (Currently, this scheduler is very simple: it simply cycles through all the runnable tasks, running each one until it hits its ~first~ next yield point. But this might change in the future.)
> 
> 2. The task resumes, and should check for cancellation. The trio scheduler at some point returns control to the task -- but things may have happened while the task was suspended. Especially, the task may have been cancelled. If that matters to you, After a yield point is a good time to check for cancellation, and potentially raise a Cancelled error. See \[Cancellation and timeouts] below for more details.

The bit about cancellation needs improvement, because from the docs I only understood how to use the `with trio.move_on_after(timeout=...)` block -- I couldn't find an example of how to check for cancellation yourself.

Hi Michelle,

I set up the multi-gas version of FAIR to give realistic results when spun
up from pre-industrial and historical emissions under the RCP scenarios, so
it may give strange results for a pulse emission of methane.

The first thing to note is that natural, time varying emissions of CH4 and
N2O are switched on by default in multigas mode and that present-day and
pre-industrial emissions are not the same. This may be why you see a
negative RF for zero emissions. Secondly there is a pre-industrial
concentration of non-CO2 gases provided which is passed to the radiative
forcing calculation. And thirdly under both the default Etminan forcing
calculation and the alternative option (Myhre) the methane forcing depends
also on concentrations of N2O. There may be other things at play which are
also affected by methane emissions (tropospheric ozone and oxidation to
CO2). You may have already done this but you may be able to find a
documentation branch on the main GitHub which gives some examples
(unfortunately I don't think it's exhaustive).

I'm currently travelling but if you can hold on until after Easter I'll
provide the combination of options you need for a pulse emission of methane
(or make code changes to enable it).

Thanks,
Chris

@houseroad , if the only op, an Identity, is removed, what is the expected behavior of this graph? There is no op, so semantically it shouldn't do anything. Is it a defined behavior of a 0-op graph in ONNX? For RNNs, CoreML can output both of the two identical outputs as a part their graph outputs. If optimization happens, we have two duplicated output names. I don't know if ONNX allows this situation. Therefore, an alternative solution is (1) to explain what users should do for empty graphs (2) clarify that duplicated graph outputs are allowed (it's confusing because operator should also produce unique output names).

@skottmckay so, if I understand correctly, the issue you're raising applies to all of LSTM/GRU/RNN in the same way. So let's look at the simplest case, RNN. It has one equation,

```
- Ht = f(Xt*(Wi^T) + Ht-1*Ri + Wbi + Rbi)
```

You are proposing to change it to

```
- Ht = f(Xt*(Wi^T) + Ht-1*(Ri^T) + Wbi + Rbi)
```

correct?

I agree that the new 3.1 is a step forward compared to 3.0 which was
causing lots of confusion.

> * PUB/SUB sockets are filtered on the *PUB* side, potentially reducing network load.

This is a huge deal.

> * durable sockets are removed (routing a message by IDENTITY to a peer you have seen before, but are not currently connected to, will no longer keep the message buffered in anticipation of a reconnect).

I think this makes more sense as it prevents messages from sitting in
queues waiting for reconnects that may never happen.

> Neither of which should have any effect on 99% of pyzmq application developers, very much unlike 3.0.

Right, but actually it works if you use model.pk when creating the list.

```python
def test_list_with_foreignkeys(self):
    class Model(models.Model):
        pass
    class ReferenceList(models.Model):
        keys = ListField(models.ForeignKey(Model))
    model1 = Model.objects.create()
    model2 = Model.objects.create()
    ReferenceList.objects.create(keys=[model1.pk, model2.pk])
    self.assertEqual(ReferenceList.objects.get().keys[0], model1.pk)
    self.assertEqual(
        Model.objects.get(pk=ReferenceList.objects.get().keys[0]),
        model1)
```

This following doesn't work with Mongo however:

```python
self.assertEqual(len(ReferenceList.objects.filter(keys=model1.pk)), 1)
```
